    <meta name="dc.description" content="A k-page upward book embedding (kUBE) of a directed acyclic graph&nbsp;G is a book embeddings of&nbsp;G on k pages with the additional requirement that the vertices appear in a topological ordering along the spine of the book. The k UBE Testing problem, which asks whether a graph admits a kUBE, was introduced in 1999 by Heath, Pemmaraju, and Trenk (SIAM J Comput 28(4), 1999). In a companion paper, Heath and Pemmaraju (SIAM J Comput 28(5), 1999) proved that the problem is linear-time solvable for $$k=1$$ and NP-complete for $$k = 6$$ . Closing this gap has been a central question in algorithmic graph theory since then. In this paper, we make a major contribution towards a definitive answer to the above question by showing that k UBE Testing is NP-complete for $$k\ge 3$$ , even for st-graphs, i.e., acyclic directed graphs with a single source and a single sink. Indeed, our result, together with a recent work of Bekos et al. (Theor Comput Sci 946, 2023) that proves the NP-completeness of 2UBE for planar st-graphs, closes the question about the complexity of the kUBE problem for any k. Motivated by this hardness result, we then focus on the 2UBE Testing for planar st-graphs. On the algorithmic side, we present an $$O(f(\beta )\cdot n+n^3)$$ -time algorithm for 2UBE Testing, where $$\beta $$ is the branchwidth of the input graph and f is a singly-exponential function on $$\beta $$ . Since the treewidth and the branchwidth of a graph are within a constant factor from each other, this result immediately yields an FPT algorithm for st-graphs of bounded treewidth. Furthermore, we describe an O(n)-time algorithm to test whether a plane st-graph whose faces have a special structure admits a 2UBE that additionally preserves the plane embedding of the input st-graph. On the combinatorial side, we present two notable families of plane st-graphs that always admit an embedding-preserving $$2$$ UBE."/>

    <meta property="og:url" content="https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y"/>
    <meta property="og:type" content="article"/>
    <meta property="og:site_name" content="SpringerLink"/>
    <meta property="og:title" content="Upward Book Embeddability of st-Graphs: Complexity and Algorithms - Algorithmica"/>
    <meta property="og:description" content="A k-page upward book embedding (kUBE) of a directed acyclic graph G is a book embeddings of G on k pages with the additional requirement that the vertices appear in a topological ordering along the spine of the book. The k UBE Testing problem, which asks whether a graph admits a kUBE, was introduced in 1999 by Heath, Pemmaraju, and Trenk (SIAM J Comput 28(4), 1999). In a companion paper, Heath and Pemmaraju (SIAM J Comput 28(5), 1999) proved that the problem is linear-time solvable for $$k=1$$ k = 1 and NP-complete for $$k = 6$$ k = 6 . Closing this gap has been a central question in algorithmic graph theory since then. In this paper, we make a major contribution towards a definitive answer to the above question by showing that k UBE Testing is NP-complete for $$k\ge 3$$ k ≥ 3 , even for st-graphs, i.e., acyclic directed graphs with a single source and a single sink. Indeed, our result, together with a recent work of Bekos et al. (Theor Comput Sci 946, 2023) that proves the NP-completeness of 2UBE for planar st-graphs, closes the question about the complexity of the kUBE problem for any k. Motivated by this hardness result, we then focus on the 2UBE Testing for planar st-graphs. On the algorithmic side, we present an $$O(f(\beta )\cdot n+n^3)$$ O ( f ( β ) · n + n 3 ) -time algorithm for 2UBE Testing, where $$\beta $$ β is the branchwidth of the input graph and f is a singly-exponential function on $$\beta $$ β . Since the treewidth and the branchwidth of a graph are within a constant factor from each other, this result immediately yields an FPT algorithm for st-graphs of bounded treewidth. Furthermore, we describe an O(n)-time algorithm to test whether a plane st-graph whose faces have a special structure admits a 2UBE that additionally preserves the plane embedding of the input st-graph. On the combinatorial side, we present two notable families of plane st-graphs that always admit an embedding-preserving $$2$$ 2 UBE."/>

    <script type="application/ld+json">{"mainEntity":{"headline":"Upward Book Embeddability of st-Graphs: Complexity and Algorithms","description":"A k-page upward book embedding (kUBE) of a directed acyclic graph G is a book embeddings of G on k pages with the additional requirement that the vertices appear in a topological ordering along the spine of the book. The k UBE Testing problem, which asks whether a graph admits a kUBE, was introduced in 1999 by Heath, Pemmaraju, and Trenk (SIAM J Comput 28(4), 1999). In a companion paper, Heath and Pemmaraju (SIAM J Comput 28(5), 1999) proved that the problem is linear-time solvable for \n                \n                  \n                \n                $$k=1$$\n                \n               and NP-complete for \n                \n                  \n                \n                $$k = 6$$\n                \n              . Closing this gap has been a central question in algorithmic graph theory since then. In this paper, we make a major contribution towards a definitive answer to the above question by showing that k UBE Testing is NP-complete for \n                \n                  \n                \n                $$k\\ge 3$$\n                \n              , even for st-graphs, i.e., acyclic directed graphs with a single source and a single sink. Indeed, our result, together with a recent work of Bekos et al. (Theor Comput Sci 946, 2023) that proves the NP-completeness of 2UBE for planar st-graphs, closes the question about the complexity of the kUBE problem for any k. Motivated by this hardness result, we then focus on the 2UBE Testing for planar st-graphs. On the algorithmic side, we present an \n                \n                  \n                \n                $$O(f(\\beta )\\cdot n+n^3)$$\n                \n              -time algorithm for 2UBE Testing, where \n                \n                  \n                \n                $$\\beta $$\n                \n               is the branchwidth of the input graph and f is a singly-exponential function on \n                \n                  \n                \n                $$\\beta $$\n                \n              . Since the treewidth and the branchwidth of a graph are within a constant factor from each other, this result immediately yields an FPT algorithm for st-graphs of bounded treewidth. Furthermore, we describe an O(n)-time algorithm to test whether a plane st-graph whose faces have a special structure admits a 2UBE that additionally preserves the plane embedding of the input st-graph. On the combinatorial side, we present two notable families of plane st-graphs that always admit an embedding-preserving \n                \n                  \n                \n                 <div data-article-body="true" data-track-component="article body" class="c-article-body">

                    
                    <section aria-labelledby="Abs1" data-title="Abstract" lang="en"><div class="c-article-section" id="Abs1-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="Abs1">Abstract</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="Abs1-content"><p>A <i>k</i>-<i>page upward book embedding</i> (<i>k</i>UBE) of a directed acyclic graph <i>G</i> is a book embeddings of <i>G</i> on <i>k</i> pages with the additional requirement that the vertices appear in a topological ordering along the spine of the book. The <i>k</i> <span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span> problem, which asks whether a graph admits a <i>k</i>UBE, was introduced in 1999 by Heath, Pemmaraju, and Trenk (SIAM J Comput 28(4), 1999). In a companion paper, Heath and Pemmaraju (SIAM J Comput 28(5), 1999) proved that the problem is linear-time solvable for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k=1\)</span> and NP-complete for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k = 6\)</span>. Closing this gap has been a central question in algorithmic graph theory since then. In this paper, we make a major contribution towards a definitive answer to the above question by showing that <i>k</i> <span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span> is NP-complete for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k\ge 3\)</span>, even for <i>st</i>-graphs, i.e., acyclic directed graphs with a single source and a single sink. Indeed, our result, together with a recent work of Bekos et al. (Theor Comput Sci 946, 2023) that proves the NP-completeness of 2UBE for planar <i>st</i>-graphs, closes the question about the complexity of the <i>k</i>UBE problem for any <i>k</i>. Motivated by this hardness result, we then focus on the 2UBE <span class="u-small-caps">Testing</span> for planar <i>st</i>-graphs. On the algorithmic side, we present an <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(f(\beta )\cdot n+n^3)\)</span>-time algorithm for 2UBE <span class="u-small-caps">Testing</span>, where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\beta \)</span> is the branchwidth of the input graph and <i>f</i> is a singly-exponential function on <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\beta \)</span>. Since the treewidth and the branchwidth of a graph are within a constant factor from each other, this result immediately yields an FPT algorithm for <i>st</i>-graphs of bounded treewidth. Furthermore, we describe an <i>O</i>(<i>n</i>)-time algorithm to test whether a plane <i>st</i>-graph whose faces have a special structure admits a 2UBE that additionally preserves the plane embedding of the input <i>st</i>-graph. On the combinatorial side, we present two notable families of plane <i>st</i>-graphs that always admit an embedding-preserving <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE.</p></div></div></section>

                                <div class="main-content">
                                    <section data-title="Introduction"><div class="c-article-section" id="Sec1-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="Sec1"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">1 </span>Introduction</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="Sec1-content"><p>A <i>k</i>-<i>page book embedding</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span> of an undirected graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G=(V,E)\)</span> consists of a vertex ordering <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi : V \leftrightarrow \{1,2,\dots ,|V |\}\)</span> and of an assignment <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\sigma : E \rightarrow \{1,\dots ,k\}\)</span> of the edges of <i>G</i> to one of <i>k</i> sets, called <i>pages</i>, so that for any two edges (<i>a</i>, <i>b</i>) and (<i>c</i>, <i>d</i>) in the same page, with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (a) < \pi (b)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (c) < \pi (d)\)</span>, we have neither <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (a) < \pi (c)\)</span> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(< \pi (b) < \pi (d)\)</span> nor <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (c) < \pi (a) \)</span> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(< \pi (d) < \pi (b)\)</span>. From a geometric perspective, a <i>k</i>-page book embedding can be represented as a drawing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi ,\sigma )\)</span> of <i>G</i> where the <i>k</i> pages correspond to <i>k</i> half-planes sharing a vertical line, called the <i>spine</i>. Each vertex <i>v</i> is a point on the spine with <i>y</i>-coordinate <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (v)\)</span>; each edge <i>e</i> is a circular arc on page <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\sigma (e)\)</span>, and no edges in the same page cross. As an example, Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig1">1</a>a represents a 3-page book embedding of the complete graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(K_6\)</span>.</p><p>For directed graphs (digraphs), Heath, Pemmaraju, and Trenk introduced a variant of <i>k</i>-page book embeddings in which all the edges are oriented in the upward direction [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 69" title="Heath, L.S., Pemmaraju, S.V., Trenk, A.N.: Stack and queue layouts of directed acyclic graphs: Part I. SIAM J. Comput. 28(4), 1510–1539 (1999)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR69" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1347">69</a>]. This implies that the input digraph is acyclic and all its vertices appear along the spine in a topological ordering. This type of book embedding for digraphs is called a <i>k</i>-<i>page upward book embedding</i> of <i>G</i> (<i>k</i><i>UBE</i> for short). An example of a 4UBE is shown in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig1">1</a>c (throughout the paper, in the figures showing a <i>k</i>UBE, the edges are oriented from bottom to top; for simplicity the orientation of the edges is not explicitly shown). Note that, if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span> is a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE and the two pages are co-planar, then <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi ,\sigma )\)</span> is an <i>upward planar drawing</i> of <i>G</i>, i.e., a crossing-free drawing with all edges monotonically increasing in the upward direction. Upward planar drawings represent a classical subject in topological graph theory [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" title="Angelini, P., Chaplick, S., Cornelsen, S., Da Lozzo, G.: On upward-planar L-drawings of graphs. In: Szeider, S., Ganian, R., Silva, A. (eds.) 47th International Symposium on Mathematical Foundations of Computer Science, MFCS 2022, August 22-26, 2022, Vienna, Austria, volume 241 of LIPIcs, pages 10:1–10:15. Schloss Dagstuhl - Leibniz-Zentrum für Informatik, (2022)" href="#ref-CR7" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1462">7</a>,<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" title="Angelini, P., Da Lozzo, G., Di Battista, G., Donato, V. D., Kindermann, P., Rote, G., Rutter, I.: Windrose planarity: embedding graphs with direction-constrained edges. AMS Trans. Algorithms 14(4), 54:1-54:24 (2018)" href="#ref-CR8" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1462_1">8</a>,<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 9" title="Angelini, P., Da Lozzo, G., Di Battista, G., Frati, F.: Strip planarity testing for embedded planar graphs. Algorithmica 77(4), 1022–1059 (2017)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR9" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1465">9</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 26" title="Bertolazzi, P., Di Battista, G., Didimo, W.: Quasi-upward planarity. Algorithmica 32(3), 474–506 (2002)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR26" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1468">26</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 27" title="Bertolazzi, P., Di Battista, G., Mannino, C., Tamassia, R.: Optimal upward planarity testing of single-source digraphs. SIAM J. Comput. 27(1), 132–169 (1998)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR27" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1472">27</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" title="Binucci, C., Didimo, W.: Computing quasi-upward planar drawings of mixed graphs. Comput. J. 59(1), 133–150 (2016)" href="#ref-CR32" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1475">32</a>,<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" title="Binucci, C., Didimo, W., Patrignani, M.: Upward and quasi-upward planarity testing of embedded mixed graphs. Theor. Comput. Sci. 526, 75–89 (2014)" href="#ref-CR33" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1475_1">33</a>,<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 34" title="Brandenburg, F.: Upward planar drawings on the standing and the rolling cylinders. Comput. Geom. 47(1), 25–41 (2014)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR34" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1478">34</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 36" title="Chaplick,S., Chimani, M., Cornelsen, S., Da Lozzo, G., Nöllenburg, M., Patrignani, M., Tollis, I.G., Wolff, A.: Planar L-drawings of directed graphs. In: Frati, F., Ma, K., (eds.) GD 2017, volume 10692 of LNCS, pages 465–478. Springer, (2017)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR36" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1481">36</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 39" title="Da Lozzo, G., Di Battista, G., Frati, F.: Extending upward planar graph drawings. Comput. Geom. 91, 101668 (2020)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR39" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1484">39</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 40" title="Da Lozzo, G., Di Battista, G., Frati, F., Patrignani, M., Roselli, V.: Upward planar morphs. Computing the page number of undirected and directed graphs is a widely studied problem, with applications in a variety of domains, including VLSI design, fault-tolerant processing, parallel process scheduling, sorting networks, parallel matrix computations [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 37" title="Chung, F.R.K., Leighton, F.T., Rosenberg, A.L.: Embedding graphs in books: a layout problem with applications to VLSI design. in the paper of Bernhart and Kainen [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 25" title="Bernhart, F., Kainen, P.C.: The book thickness of a graph. J. Combinat. Theory, Series B 27(3), 320–331 (1979)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR25" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1664">25</a>]. They prove that the graphs with page number one are exactly the outerplanar graphs, while graphs with page number two are the sub-Hamiltonian graphs. This second result implies that it is NP-complete to decide whether a graph admits a 2-page book embedding [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 91" title="Wigderson, A.: The complexity of the Hamiltonian circuit problem for maximal planar graphs. Technical report, 298, EECS Department, Princeton University, (1982)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR91" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1667">91</a>]. Yannakakis [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 93" title="Yannakakis, M.: Embedding planar graphs in four pages. J. Comput. Syst. Sci. 38(1), 36–67 (1989)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR93" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1670">93</a>] proved that every planar graph has a 4-page book embedding, while the question whether four pages are sometimes necessary for planar graphs has only been recently settled in the affirmative [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 24" title="Bekos, M.A., Kaufmann, M., Klute, F., Pupyrev, S., Raftopoulou, C.N., Ueckerdt, T.: Four pages are indeed necessary for planar graphs. J. Comput. J. Comb. Theory, Ser. B, 145:241–263, (2020)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR94" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1676">94</a>]. Math. 12(3), 337–341 (1999)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR56" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1806">56</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 57" title="Enomoto, H., Miyauchi, M.S., Ota, K.: Lower bounds for the number of edge-crossings over the spine in a topological book embedding of a graph. Discret. Appl. Math. 92(2–3), 149–155 (1999)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR57" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1809">57</a>]). Finally, 2-page (topological) book embeddings find applications to point-set embedding and universal point set (e.g., [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 15" title="Angelini, P., Eppstein, D., Frati, F., Kaufmann, M., Lazard, S., Mchedlidze, T., Teillaud, M., Wolff, A.: Universal point sets for drawing planar graphs with circular arcs. J. Graph Algorithms Appl. 18(3), 313–324 (2014)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR15" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1812">15</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 16" title="Badent, M., Di Giacomo, E., Liotta, G.: Drawing colored graphs on colored points. Theor. Comput. Sci. 408(2–3), 129–142 (2008)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR16" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1815">16</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 48" title="Di Giacomo, E., Liotta, G., Trotta, F.: On embedding a graph on two sets of points. Int. J. Found. Comput. Algorithmica 57(4), 796–818 (2010)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR49" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1822">49</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 59" title="Everett, H., Lazard, S., Liotta, G., Wismath, S.K.: Universal sets of
