In: Graph Drawing, volume 9411 of LNCS, pages 423–429. Springer, (2015)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR75" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1828">75</a>]).</p><p><b>Book embeddings of directed graphs.</b> As for undirected graphs, there are many papers devoted to the study of upper and lower bounds on the page number of directed graphs. Heath, Pemmaraju, and Trenk [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 69" title="Heath, L.S., Pemmaraju, S.V., Trenk, A.N.: Stack and queue layouts of directed acyclic graphs: Part I. SIAM J. Comput. 28(4), 1510–1539 (1999)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR69" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1837">69</a>] show that directed trees and unicyclic digraphs have page number one and two, respectively. Alzohairi and Rival [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 5" title="Alzohairi, M., Rival, I.: Series-parallel planar ordered sets have pagenumber two. In: S. C. North, editor, Graph Drawing, GD ’96, volume 1190 of LNCS, pp. 11–24. Springer, (1996)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR5" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1840">5</a>], and later Di Giacomo et al. [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 46" title="Di Giacomo, E., Didimo, W., Liotta, G., Wismath, S.K.: Book embeddability of series-parallel digraphs. Algorithmica 45(4), 531–547 (2006)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR46" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1843">46</a>] with an improved linear-time construction, show that series-parallel digraphs have page number two. Mchedlidze and Symvonis [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 79" title="Mchedlidze, T., Symvonis, A.: Crossing-free acyclic hamiltonian path completion for planar 
                  
                    
                  
                  $${st}$$
                  
                    
                      st
                    
                  
                -digraphs. In: Dong, Y., Du, D., Ibarra, O.H., (eds.) Algorithms and Computation, ISAAC 2009, volume 5878 of LNCS, pages 882–891. Springer, (2009)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR79" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1846">79</a>] generalize this result and prove that <i>N</i>-free upward planar digraphs, which include series-parallel digraphs, also have page number two (a digraph is upward planar if it admits an upward planar drawing). Bhore et al. [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 28" title="Bhore, S., Da Lozzo, G., Montecchiani, F., Nöllenburg, M.: On the upward book thickness problem: combinatorial and complexity results. Eur. J. Comb. 110, 103662 (2023)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR28" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1853">28</a>] provide constant upper bounds on the page number of several subfamilies of upward outerplanar graphs. Frati et al. [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 61" title="Frati, F., Fulek, R., Ruiz-Vargas, A.J.: On the page number of upward planar directed acyclic graphs. J. Graph Algorithms and Appl. 17(3), 221–244 (2013)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR61" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1856">61</a>] give several conditions under which upward planar triangulations have bounded page number. Recently, Jungeblut, Merker, and Ueckerdt [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 73" title="Jungeblut, P., Merker, L., Ueckerdt, T.: A sublinear bound on the page number of upward planar graphs. In: J. S. Naor and N. Buchbinder, editors, Proceedings of the 2022 ACM-SIAM Symposium on Discrete Algorithms, SODA 2022, Virtual Conference / Alexandria, VA, USA, January 9 - 12, 2022, pages 963–978. SIAM, (2022)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR73" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1859">73</a>] proved the first sublinear upper bound on the page number of upward planar graphs. Overall, the question asked by Nowakowski and Parker [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 82" title="Nowakowski, R., Parker, A.: Ordered sets, pagenumbers and planarity. Order 6(3), 209–218 (1989)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR82" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1862">82</a>] more than 30 years ago of whether the page number of planar posets, and more generally of upward planar digraphs, is bounded remains unanswered. The page number of acyclic digraphs in terms of posets is also investigated by Alhashem, Jourdan, and Zaguia [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 4" title="Alhashem, M., Jourdan, G., Zaguia, N.: On the book embedding of ordered sets. Ars Combin. 119, 47–64 (2015)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR4" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1865">4</a>].</p><p>About lower bounds, Nowakowski and Parker [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 82" title="Nowakowski, R., Parker, A.: Ordered sets, pagenumbers and planarity. Order 6(3), 209–218 (1989)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR82" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1871">82</a>] give an example of an upward planar digraph that requires 3 pages in any upward book embedding (see Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig10">10</a>a). Subsequently, Hung [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 72" title="Hung, L.T.Q.: A Planar Poset which Requires 4 Pages. PhD thesis, Institute of Computer Science, University of Wrocław, (1989)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR72" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1877">72</a>] shows an upward planar digraph with page number 4. This bounds has been improved to 5 pages by Jungeblut, Merker, and Ueckerdt [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 73" title="Jungeblut, P., Merker, L., Ueckerdt, T.: A sublinear bound on the page number of upward planar graphs. In: J. S. Naor and N. Buchbinder, editors, Proceedings of the 2022 ACM-SIAM Symposium on Discrete Algorithms, SODA 2022, Virtual Conference / Alexandria, VA, USA, January 9 - 12, 2022, pages 963–978. SIAM, (2022)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR73" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1880">73</a>]. Heath and Pemmaraju [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 67" title="Heath, L.S., Pemmaraju, S.V.: Stack and queue layouts of posets. SIAM J. Dis. Math. 10(4), 599–625 (1997)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR67" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1883">67</a>] describe acyclic <i>n</i>-vertex planar digraphs that are not upward planar and whose upward book embeddings require <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\lfloor n/2\rfloor \)</span> pages. Syslo [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 88" title="Syslo, M.M.: Bounds to the page number of partially ordered sets. In: Nagl, M., (ed.) Graph-Theoretic Concepts in Computer Science, WG ’89, volume 411 of LNCS, pp. 181–195. Springer, (1989)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR88" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1919">88</a>] provides a lower bound on the page number of a poset in terms of its bump number.</p><p>Besides the study of upper and lower bounds on the page number of digraphs, several papers concentrate on the design of testing algorithms for the existence of <i>k</i>UBEs. Prior to this paper, the problem was known to be NP-complete only for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k=6\)</span> [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 68" title="Heath, L.S., Pemmaraju, S.V.: Stack and queue layouts of directed acyclic graphs: Part II. SIAM J. Comput. 28(5), 1588–1626 (1999)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR68" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1952">68</a>]. Only very recently, the case <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k=2\)</span> has been settled by Bekos et al. [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 19" title="Bekos, M.A., Da Lozzo, G., Frati, F., Gronemann, M., Mchedlidze, T., Raftopoulou, C.N.: Recognizing dags with page-number 2 is NP-complete. Theor. Comput. Sci. 946, 113689 (2023)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR19" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e1979">19</a>], who show that the 2UBE problem is NP-complete for planar <i>st</i>-graphs and planar posets<sup><a href="#Fn1"><span class="u-visually-hidden">Footnote </span>1</a></sup>. Furthermore, for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k=2\)</span>, Mchedlidze and Symvonis give linear-time testing algorithms for outerplanar and planar triangulated <i>st</i>-graphs [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 81" title="Mchedlidze, T., Symvonis, A.: Crossing-optimal acyclic HP-completion for outerplanar st-digraphs. J. Graph Algorithms and Appl. 15(3), 373–415 (2011)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR81" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e2028">81</a>]. They also present an <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(w^2n^w)\)</span>-time testing algorithm for 2UBEs of <i>n</i>-vertex planar <i>st</i>-graphs of width <i>w</i>, where the <i>width</i> is the minimum number of directed paths that cover all the vertices [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 79" title="Mchedlidze, T., Symvonis, A.: Crossing-free acyclic hamiltonian path completion for planar 
                  
                    
                  
                  $${st}$$
                  
                    
                      st
                    
                  
                -digraphs. In: Dong, Y., Du, D., Ibarra, O.H., (eds.) Algorithms and Computation, ISAAC 2009, volume 5878 of LNCS, pages 882–891. Springer, (2009)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR79" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e2084">79</a>]. Heath and Pemmaraju [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 68" title="Heath, L.S., Pemmaraju, S.V.: Stack and queue layouts of directed acyclic graphs: Part II. SIAM J. Comput. 28(5), 1588–1626 (1999)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR68" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e2087">68</a>] describe a linear-time algorithm to recognize digraphs that admit 1UBEs. As for the undirected case, constrained or relaxed variants of <i>k</i>UBEs for digraphs are studied [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 2" title="Akitaya, H.A. , Demaine, E.D., Hesterberg, A., Liu, Q.C.: Upward partitioned book embeddings. In: F. Frati and K. Ma, editors, GD 2017, volume 10692 of LNCS, pp. 210–223. Springer, (2017)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR2" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e2094">2</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 47" title="Di Giacomo, E., Giordano, F., Liotta, G.: Upward topological book embeddings of dags. SIAM J. Dis. Math. 25(2), 479–489 (2011)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR47" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e2097">47</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 64" title="Giordano, F., Liotta, G., Mchedlidze, T., Symvonis, A., Whitesides, S.: Computing upward topological book embeddings of upward planar digraphs. J. Dis. Algorithms 30, 45–69 (2015)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR64" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e2100">64</a>], as well as applications to the point-set embedding problem [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 46" title="Di Giacomo, E., Didimo, W., Liotta, G., Wismath, S.K.: Book embeddability of series-parallel digraphs. Algorithmica 45(4), 531–547 (2006)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR46" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e2103">46</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 64" title="Giordano, F., Liotta, G., Mchedlidze, T., Symvonis, A., Whitesides, S.: Computing upward topological book embeddings of upward planar digraphs. J. Dis. Algorithms 30, 45–69 (2015)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR64" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e2106">64</a>].</p><p><b>Contributions.</b> Our paper is motivated by the gap present in the literature about the computational complexity of the <i>k</i> <span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span> problem. Namely, as discussed above, polynomial-time algorithms are known only for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k=1\)</span>, or for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k=2\)</span> and subclasses of planar digraphs, while NP-completeness is known only for exactly 6 pages. Closing this gap has been a central question in algorithmic graph theory for over 20 years. In this paper, we make a major step towards a definitive answer to the above question, and address the research direction proposed by Heath and Pemmaraju [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 68" title="Heath, L.S., Pemmaraju, S.V.: Stack and queue layouts of directed acyclic graphs: Part II. SIAM J. Comput. 28(5), 1588–1626 (1999)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR68" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e2168">68</a>] about identifying graph classes for which the existence of <i>k</i>UBEs can be solved efficiently. Our results are as follows:</p><ul class="u-list-style-bullet">
                <li>
                  <p>We prove that testing whether a digraph <i>G</i> admits a <i>k</i>UBE is NP-complete for every <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k \ge 3\)</span>, even if <i>G</i> is an <i>st</i>-graph (Section <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Sec3">3</a>). An analogous result was previously known only for the constrained version in which the page assignment is given [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 2" title="Akitaya, H.A. , Demaine, E.D., Hesterberg, A., Liu, Q.C.: Upward partitioned book embeddings. In: F. Frati and K. Ma, editors, GD 2017, volume 10692 of LNCS, pp. 210–223. Springer, (2017)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR2" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e2221">2</a>]. Our result and the recent work of Bekos et al. [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 19" title="Bekos, M.A., Da Lozzo, G., Frati, F., Gronemann, M., Mchedlidze, T., Raftopoulou, C.N.: Recognizing dags with page-number 2 is NP-complete. Theor. Comput. Sci. 946, 113689 (2023)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR19" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e2224">19</a>] close the question about the complexity of the <i>k</i>UBE problem for any <i>k</i>.</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p>We describe a new meaningful subclass of upward planar digraphs that admit a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE (Section <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Sec4">4</a>). This class is structurally different from the <i>N</i>-free upward planar digraphs, the largest class of upward 2-page book embeddable digraphs previously known.</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p>We give a linear-time algorithm to test the existence of an embedding-preserving <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE for a family of plane <i>st</i>-graphs whose faces have a special structure, namely each face is either a generalized triangle (i.e., it contains a transitive edge) or a rhombus (i.e., its left and right paths have length two); see Section <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Sec5">5</a>.</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p>For planar <i>n</i>-vertex <i>st</i>-graphs of bounded branchwidth <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\mathrm{\beta }\,}}\)</span>, we describe an FPT algorithm for the <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE <span class="u-small-caps">Testing</span> problem that runs in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(f({{\,\mathrm{\beta }\,}})\cdot n + n^3)\)</span> time, where <i>f</i> is a singly-exponential function (Section <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Sec6">6</a>). The algorithm works for both variable and fixed embedding, and it implies a sub-exponential-time algorithm for general planar <i>st</i>-graphs. Since the treewidth and the branchwidth of a graph are within a constant factor from each other, this result immediately yields an FPT algorithm for <i>st</i>-graphs of bounded treewidth.</p>
                </li>
                <li>
                  <p>The approach used in the design of the FPT algorithm also leads to an <i>O</i>(<i>n</i>)-time testing algorithm for <i>st</i>-graphs of treewidth at most two (also known as two-terminal series-parallel graphs) in the fixed embedding setting (Theorem <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar29">7</a>). We recall that in the variable embedding setting an <i>st</i>-graphs of treewidth at most two always admits a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE, which can be found in <i>O</i>(<i>n</i>) time [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 46" title="Di Giacomo, E., Didimo, W., Liotta, G., Wismath, S.K.: Book embeddability of series-parallel digraphs. Algorithmica 45(4), 531–547 (2006)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR46" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e2477">46</a>].</p>
                </li>
              </ul></div></div></section><section data-title="Preliminaries"><div class="c-article-section" id="Sec2-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="Sec2"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">2 </span>Preliminaries</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="Sec2-content"><p>We only consider (di)graphs without loops and multiple edges, and we denote by <i>V</i>(<i>G</i>) and <i>E</i>(<i>G</i>) the sets of vertices and edges of a (di)graph <i>G</i>. A graph <i>G</i> is 1-<i>connected</i> if there is a path between any two vertices. <i>G</i> is <i>k</i>-<i>connected</i>, for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k \ge 2\)</span>, if the removal of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k-1\)</span> vertices leaves the graph 1-connected. A 2-connected (3-connected) graph is also called <i>biconnected</i> (<i>triconnected</i>).</p><p>A <i>planar drawing</i> of <i>G</i> is a geometric representation in the plane such that: (<i>i</i>) each vertex <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v \in V(G)\)</span> is drawn as a distinct point <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_v\)</span>; (<i>ii</i>) each edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\(e=(u,v) \in E(G)\)</span> is drawn as a simple curve connecting <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_u\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_v\)</span>; (<i>iii</i>) no two edges intersect in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma \)</span> except at their common end-vertices (if they are adjacent). A graph is <i>planar</i> if it admits a planar drawing. A planar drawing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma \)</span> of <i>G</i> divides the plane into topologically connected regions, called <i>faces</i>. The <i>outer face</i> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma \)</span> is the region of unbounded size; the other faces are <i>internal</i>. A <i>planar embedding</i> of <i>G</i> is an equivalence class of planar drawings that define the same set of (internal and outer) faces, and it can be described by the clockwise sequence of vertices and edges on the boundary of each face plus the choice of the outer face. Graph <i>G</i> together with a given planar embedding is an <i>embedded planar graph</i>, or simply a <i>plane graph</i>: If <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma \)</span> is a planar drawing of <i>G</i> whose set of faces is described by the planar embedding of <i>G</i>, we say that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma \)</span> <i>preserves</i> this embedding, or also that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma \)</span> is an <i>embedding-preserving drawing</i> of <i>G</i>.</p><p>A digraph <i>G</i> is a <i>planar </i><i>st</i>-<i>graph</i> if: (<i>i</i>) it is acyclic; (<i>ii</i>) it has a single source <i>s</i> and a single sink <i>t</i>; (<i>iii</i>) it admits a planar embedding <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {E}}\)</span> with <i>s</i> and <i>t</i> on the outer face. A planar <i>st</i>-graph <i>G</i> together with <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {E}}\)</span> is a <i>planar embedded </i><i>st</i>-<i>graph</i> or a <i>plane </i><i>st</i>-<i>graph</i>.</p><p>Let <i>G</i> be a plane <i>st</i>-graph and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(e=(u,v)\)</span> be an edge of <i>G</i>. The <i>left face</i> (resp. <i>right face</i>) of <i>e</i> is the face to the left (resp. right) of <i>e</i> while moving from <i>u</i> to <i>v</i>. The boundary of every face <i>f</i> of <i>G</i> consists of two directed paths <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_l\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_r\)</span> from a common source <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_f\)</span> to a common sink <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_f\)</span>. The paths <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_l\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_r\)</span> are the <i>left path</i> and the <i>right path</i> of <i>f</i>, respectively. The vertices <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_f\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_f\)</span> are the <i>source</i> and the <i>sink</i> of <i>f</i>, respectively. If <i>f</i> is the outer face, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_l\)</span> (resp. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_r\)</span>) consists of the edges for which <i>f</i> is the left face (resp. right face); in this case <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_l\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_r\)</span> are also called the <i>left boundary</i> and the <i>right boundary</i> of <i>G</i>, respectively. If <i>f</i> is an internal face, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_l\)</span> (resp. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_r\)</span>) consists of the edges for which <i>f</i> is the right face (resp. left face).</p><p>The <i>dual graph</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G^*\)</span> of a plane <i>st</i>-graph <i>G</i> is a plane <i>st</i>-graph (possibly with multiple edges) such that: (i) <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G^*\)</span> has a vertex associated with each internal face of <i>G</i> and two vertices <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s^*\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t^*\)</span> associated with the outer face of <i>G</i>, that are the source and the sink of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G^*\)</span>, respectively; (ii) for each internal edge <i>e</i> of <i>G</i>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G^*\)</span> has a dual edge from the left to the right face of <i>e</i>; (iii) for each edge <i>e</i> in the left boundary of <i>G</i>, there is an edge from <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s^*\)</span> to the right face of <i>e</i>; and (iv) for each edge <i>e</i> in the right boundary of <i>G</i>, there is an edge from the left face of <i>e</i> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t^*\)</span>.</p><div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-2" data-title="Fig. 2"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig2" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 2</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/2" rel="nofollow"><picture><source type="image/webp" srcset="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig2_HTML.png?as=webp"><img aria-describedby="figure-2-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig2_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 2" loading="lazy" width="685" height="258"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-2-desc"><p><b>a</b> A plane <i>st</i>-graph <i>G</i>. <b>b</b> The dual of <i>G</i> is shown in gray. <b>c</b> An embedding-preserving HP-completion of <i>G</i>; the light blue edges form a directed Hamiltonian <i>st</i>-path, and the dashed edges are augmenting edges. <b>d</b> An embedding-preserving <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <i>G</i> corresponding to (<b>c</b>)</p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/2" data-track-dest="link:Figure2 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 2" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div><p>Consider a planar <i>st</i>-graph <i>G</i> and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}\)</span> be a planar <i>st</i>-graph obtained by augmenting <i>G</i> with directed edges in such a way that it contains a directed Hamiltonian <i>st</i>-path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}}\)</span>. The graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}\)</span> is an <i>HP-completion</i> of <i>G</i>. Consider now a plane <i>st</i>-graph <i>G</i> and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {E}}\)</span> be a planar embedding of <i>G</i>. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}\)</span> be an embedded HP-completion of <i>G</i> whose embedding <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\overline{{\mathcal {E}}}\)</span> is such that its restriction to <i>G</i> is <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {E}}\)</span>. We say that <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}\)</span> is an <i>embedding-preserving</i> <i>HP-completion</i> of <i>G</i>. Bernhart and Kainen [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 25" title="Bernhart, F., Kainen, P.C.: The book thickness of a graph. J. Combinat. Theory, Series B 27(3), 320–331 (1979)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR25" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e3945">25</a>] prove that an undirected planar graph admits a 2-page book embedding if and only if it is <i>sub-Hamiltonian</i>, i.e., it can be made Hamiltonian by adding edges while preserving its planarity. Theorem <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar1">1</a> is an immediate consequence of the result in [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 25" title="Bernhart, F., Kainen, P.C.: The book thickness of a graph. J. Combinat. Theory, Series B 27(3), 320–331 (1979)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR25" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e3954">25</a>] for planar digraphs (see also Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig2">2</a>); when we say that a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi ,\sigma \rangle \)</span> is <i>embedding-preserving</i> we mean that the drawing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi ,\sigma )\)</span> preserves the planar embedding of <i>G</i>.</p>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar1">Theorem 1</h3>
                <p>A planar (plane) <i>st</i>-graph <i>G</i> admits a (embedding-preserving) <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi ,\sigma \rangle \)</span> if and only if <i>G</i> admits an (embedding-preserving) HP-completion <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}\)</span>. Also, the order <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span> coincides with the order of the vertices along <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}}\)</span>.</p>
              </div></div></section><section data-title="NP-Completeness for kUBE (\(k \ge 3\))"><div class="c-article-section" id="Sec3-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="Sec3"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">3 </span>NP-Completeness for <i>k</i>UBE (<span class="mathjax-tex">\(k \ge 3\)</span>)</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="Sec3-content"><p>In this section, we prove that <i>k</i> <span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span>, i.e., the problem of deciding whether a digraph <i>G</i> admits an upward <i>k</i>-page book embedding, is NP-complete for each fixed <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k \ge 3\)</span>. The proof uses a reduction from the <span class="u-small-caps">Betweenness</span> problem [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 83" title="Opatrny, J.: Total ordering problem. SIAM J. Comput. 8(1), 111–114 (1979)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR83" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e4261">83</a>], which is defined as follows:</p><p><span class="u-small-caps">Betweenness</span></p><blockquote class="c-blockquote"><div class="c-blockquote__body">
                 <dl class="c-abbreviation_list"><dt class="c-abbreviation_list__term u-text-bold u-float-left u-pr-16" style="min-width:50px;"><dfn><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textit{Instance:}\)</span>:</dfn></dt><dd class="c-abbreviation_list__description u-mb-24">
                      <p>A finite set <i>S</i> of elements and a set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(R \subseteq S \times S \times S\)</span> of triplets.</p>
                    </dd><dt class="c-abbreviation_list__term u-text-bold u-float-left u-pr-16" style="min-width:50px;"><dfn><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textit{Question:}\)</span>:</dfn></dt><dd class="c-abbreviation_list__description u-mb-24">
                      <p>Does there exist an ordering <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\tau : S \rightarrow {\mathbb {N}}\)</span> of the elements of <i>S</i> such that for any element <span class="mathjax-tex">\((a,b,c) \in R\)</span> either <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\tau (a)< \tau (b) <\tau (c)\)</span> or <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\tau (c)< \tau (b) < \tau (a)\)</span>?</p>
                    </dd></dl>
              </div></blockquote><p>We incrementally define a set of families of digraphs and prove some properties of these digraphs. Then, we use the digraphs of these families to reduce a generic instance of <span class="u-small-caps">Betweenness</span> to an instance of 3<span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span>, thus proving the hardness result for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k=3\)</span>. We then explain how the proof can be easily adapted to work for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k>3\)</span>.</p><p>For a digraph <i>G</i>, we denote by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u \leadsto v\)</span> a directed path from a vertex <i>u</i> to a vertex <i>v</i> in <i>G</i>. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma =\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span> be a 3UBE of <i>G</i>. Two edges (<i>u</i>, <i>v</i>) and (<i>w</i>, <i>z</i>) of <i>G</i> <i>conflict</i> if either <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (u)< \pi (w)< \pi (v) < \pi (z)\)</span> or <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (w)< \pi (u)< \pi (z) < \pi (v)\)</span>. Two conflicting edges cannot be assigned to the same page. The next property will be used in the following; it is immediate from the definition of book embedding and from the pigeonhole principle.</p>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar2">Property 1</h3>
                <p>In a 3UBE there cannot exist 4 edges that mutually conflict.</p>
              <p><b>Shell digraphs.</b> The first family that we define are the <i>shell digraphs</i>, recursively defined as follows. Digraph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_0\)</span>, depicted in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig3">3</a>a, consists of a directed path <i>P</i> with eight vertices denoted as <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_0\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(q_0\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_{-1}\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{-1}\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s'_0\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(q'_0\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'_0\)</span>, and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_0\)</span> in the order they appear along <i>P</i>. Besides the edges of <i>P</i>, the following directed edges exists in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_0\)</span>: <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_0,s'_0)\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\((q_0,q'_0)\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t_{-1},p_0)\)</span>. Finally, there is a vertex <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_0\)</span> connected to <i>P</i> by means of the two directed edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{-1},t_0)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t'_0,t_0)\)</span>. Graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span> is obtained from <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{h-1}\)</span> with additional vertices and edges as shown in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig3">3</a>b. A new directed path of two vertices <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_h\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(q_h\)</span> is connected to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{h-1}\)</span> with the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((q_h,s_{h-1})\)</span>; a second path of four vertices <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s'_{h}\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(q'_{h}\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'_{h}\)</span>, and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_h\)</span> is connected to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span> with the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t_{h-1},s'_h)\)</span>. The following edges exist between these new vertices: <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_h,s'_h)\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\((q_h,q'_h)\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t_{h-1},p_h)\)</span>. Finally, there is a vertex <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_h\)</span> connected to the other vertices by means of the two directed edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{h-1},t_h)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t'_h,t_h)\)</span>. For any <span class="mathjax-tex">\(h \ge 0\)</span>, the edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_h,s'_h)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((q_h,q'_h)\)</span> are called the <i>forcing edges</i> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span>; the edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{h-1},t_h)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t_{h-1},p_h)\)</span> are the <i>channel edges</i> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span>; the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t'_h,t_h)\)</span> is the <i>closing edge</i> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span>. The vertices and edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h \setminus G_{h-1}\)</span> are the <i>exclusive vertices and edges</i> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span>. The following lemma establishes basic properties of the shell digraphs.</p><div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-3" data-title="Fig. 3"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig3" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 3</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/3" rel="nofollow"><picture><img aria-describedby="figure-3-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig3_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 3" loading="lazy" width="685" height="606"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-3-desc"><p>Definition of shell digraphs</p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/3" data-track-dest="link:Figure3 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 3" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar3">Lemma 1</h3>
                <p>Every shell digraph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span> for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(h \ge 0\)</span> admits a 3UBE. In any 3UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma =\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span> the following conditions hold for every <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=0,1,\dots ,h\)</span>: </p><dl class="c-abbreviation_list"><dt class="c-abbreviation_list__term u-text-bold u-float-left u-pr-16" style="min-width:50px;"><dfn><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textsf {S1}\)</span>:</dfn></dt><dd class="c-abbreviation_list__description u-mb-24">
                      <p>all vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_i\)</span> are between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_i\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_i\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>;</p>
                    </dd><dt class="c-abbreviation_list__term u-text-bold u-float-left u-pr-16" style="min-width:50px;"><dfn><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textsf {S2}\)</span>:</dfn></dt><dd class="c-abbreviation_list__description u-mb-24">
                      <p>the channel edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_i\)</span> are in the same page;</p>
                    </dd><dt class="c-abbreviation_list__term u-text-bold u-float-left u-pr-16" style="min-width:50px;"><dfn><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textsf {S3}\)</span>:</dfn></dt><dd class="c-abbreviation_list__description u-mb-24">
                      <p>if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i > 0\)</span>, the channel edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_i\)</span> and those of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{i-1}\)</span> are in different pages.</p>
                    </dd></dl>
              
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar4">Proof</h3>
                <p>The proof is by induction on <i>h</i>.</p>
                <p><b>Base case </b> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(h=0\)</span>. We describe how to define a 3UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma =\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_0\)</span>. The eight vertices of the directed path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_0 \leadsto p_0\)</span> must appear in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span> in the same order they appear along the path. Consider now <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_0\)</span>. Because of the closing edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t'_0,t_0)\)</span>, we have <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (t'_0) < \pi (t_0)\)</span>. If we put <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_0\)</span> between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'_0\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_0\)</span>, the channel edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{-1},t_0)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t_{-1},p_0)\)</span> and the forcing edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_{0},s'_0)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((q_{0},q'_0)\)</span> would mutually conflict. But then a 3UBE would not exist by Property <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar2">1</a>. Thus, the only possibility is that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_0\)</span> is the last vertex in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>. This uniquely defines the order <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span> and implies condition <span class="u-sans-serif">S1</span>. As for the page assignment <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\sigma \)</span>, the two forcing edges must be in different pages because they conflict. Since each of the two channel edges conflicts with both forcing edges, the channel edges cannot be assigned to the pages used for the forcing edges. Thus, they must be in the same page, which is possible because the two channel edges do not conflict (this proves condition <span class="u-sans-serif">S2</span>). Finally, the closing edge conflicts with the channel edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t_{-1},p_0)\)</span> and thus it cannot be in the same page as the channel edges; since however it does not conflict with any other edge it can be assigned to one of the pages used for the forcing edges. This concludes the proof that a 3UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_0\)</span> exists and that it must satisfy conditions <span class="u-sans-serif">S1</span> and <span class="u-sans-serif">S2</span>. Condition <span class="u-sans-serif">S3</span> does not apply in this case.</p>
                <p><b>Inductive case </b> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(h>0\)</span>. By induction, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{h-1}\)</span> admits a 3UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma '=\langle \pi ', \sigma ' \rangle \)</span> that satisfies <span class="u-sans-serif">S1</span>–<span class="u-sans-serif">S3</span>. We extend <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma '\)</span> to a 3UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma =\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span> as follows. Since <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma '\)</span> satisfies <span class="u-sans-serif">S1</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_{h-1}\)</span> is the first vertex in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi '\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{h-1}\)</span> is the last one. The vertices of path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_h \leadsto s_{h-1}\)</span> must appear in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span> in the same order they appear along the path. Analogously, the vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{h-1} \leadsto p_h\)</span> must appear in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span> in the order they have along the path. Because of the closing edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t'_h,t_h)\)</span>, we have <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (t'_h) < \pi (t_h)\)</span>. Therefore, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_h\)</span> must be the first vertex along <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>. Consider now <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_h\)</span>. If we put <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_h\)</span> between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'_h\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_h\)</span>, the channel edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{h-1},t_h)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t_{h-1},p_h)\)</span> and the forcing edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_{h},s'_h)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((q_{h},q'_h)\)</span> would mutually conflict. But then a 3UBE would not exist by Property <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar2">1</a>. Thus, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_h\)</span> must be the last vertex in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>. This uniquely defines the order <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span> and implies condition <span class="u-sans-serif">S1</span> for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span>. As for the page assignment <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\sigma \)</span>, observe that the only exclusive edge of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span> that conflicts with some edge of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{h-1}\)</span> is the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{h-1},t_h)\)</span>, which only conflicts with the channel edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{h-2},t_{h-1})\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{h-1}\)</span>. This implies that <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{h-1},t_h)\)</span> must be in a page different from the one of <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{h-2},t_{h-1})\)</span>. The two forcing edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span> must be in a page different from the channel edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{h-1},t_h)\)</span> and since they conflict, they must be in different pages. The channel edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t_{h-1},p_h)\)</span> conflicts with the forcing edges but not with the other channel edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{h-1},t_h)\)</span>. Thus, the channel edges must be in the same page (which proves condition <span class="u-sans-serif">S2</span>). The fact that the page of <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{h-1},t_h)\)</span> must be different from that of <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{h-2},t_{h-1})\)</span>, implies condition <span class="u-sans-serif">S3</span>. Finally, the closing edge conflicts with the channel edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t_{h-1},p_h)\)</span> and thus it cannot be in the same page as the channel edges; since however it does not conflict with any other edge, it can be assigned to one of the pages used for the forcing edges. This concludes the proof that a 3UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span> exists and that it satisfies conditions <span class="u-sans-serif">S1</span>, <span class="u-sans-serif">S2</span>, and <span class="u-sans-serif">S3</span>. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
              <p>Note that Condition <span class="u-sans-serif">S1</span> uniquely defines the vertex ordering of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span> in every 3UBE. Namely, the path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_h \leadsto p_0\)</span> precedes each path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{i-1} \leadsto p_i\)</span> (for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=1,\dots ,h\)</span>), and each path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{i-1} \leadsto p_i\)</span> precedes the path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{i} \leadsto p_{i+1}\)</span> (for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=1,\dots ,h-1\)</span>) (see Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig4">4</a>a for an example with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(h=2\)</span>).</p><div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-4" data-title="Fig. 4"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig4" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 4</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/4" rel="nofollow"><picture><source type="image/webp" srcset="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig4_HTML.png?as=webp"><img aria-describedby="figure-4-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig4_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 4" loading="lazy" width="685" height="608"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-4-desc"><p><b>a</b> A 3UBE of the shell digraph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_2\)</span>; the colors of the edges represent the pages. <b>b</b> Definition of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H_{h,s}\)</span> for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(h=2\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s=5\)</span></p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/4" data-track-dest="link:Figure4 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 4" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div><p><b>Filled shell digraphs.</b> Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span> be a shell digraph. A <i>filled shell digraph</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H_{h,s}\)</span> (for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(h \ge 0\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s \ge 1\)</span>) is obtained from <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span> by adding <span class="mathjax-tex">\(h+2\)</span> groups <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{-1}, \alpha _0, \dots , \alpha _h\)</span> of <i>s</i> vertices each; see Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig4">4</a>b for an illustration. The vertices of group <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _i\)</span> are denoted as <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{i,1}, v_{i,2}, \dots v_{i,s}\)</span>. These vertices will be used to map the elements of the set <i>S</i> of an instance of <span class="u-small-caps">Betweenness</span> to an instance of 3<span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span>. For each vertex <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{-1,j}\)</span> of the set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{-1}\)</span> there is a directed edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{-1},v_{-1,j})\)</span> and a directed edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{-1,j},t_{-1})\)</span>. For each vertex <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{i,j}\)</span> of the set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _i\)</span> with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i \ge 0\)</span> and <i>i</i> even, there is a directed edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_i,v_{i,j})\)</span>. Finally, for each vertex <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{i,j}\)</span> of the set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _i\)</span> with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i \ge 0\)</span>, there is a directed edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{i-1,j},v_{i,j})\)</span>.</p>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar5">Lemma 2</h3>
                <p>Every filled shell digraph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H_{h,s}\)</span> for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s>0\)</span> and even <span class="mathjax-tex">\(h \ge 0\)</span> admits a 3UBE. In any 3UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma =\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H_{h,s}\)</span> the following conditions hold for every <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=-1,0,1,\dots ,h\)</span>: </p><dl class="c-abbreviation_list"><dt class="c-abbreviation_list__term u-text-bold u-float-left u-pr-16" style="min-width:50px;"><dfn><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textsf {F1}\)</span>:</dfn></dt><dd class="c-abbreviation_list__description u-mb-24">
                      <p>the vertices of the group <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{i}\)</span> are between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_{i}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{i}\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>;</p>
                    </dd><dt class="c-abbreviation_list__term u-text-bold u-float-left u-pr-16" style="min-width:50px;"><dfn><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textsf {F2}\)</span>:</dfn></dt><dd class="c-abbreviation_list__description u-mb-24">
                      <p>if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i \ge 0\)</span> each edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{i-1,j},v_{i,j})\)</span> is in the page of the channel edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_i\)</span> (for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(j=1,\dots ,s\)</span>);</p>
                    </dd><dt class="c-abbreviation_list__term u-text-bold u-float-left u-pr-16" style="min-width:50px;"><dfn><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textsf {F3}\)</span>:</dfn></dt><dd class="c-abbreviation_list__description u-mb-24">
                      <p>if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i \ge 0\)</span> the vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{i}\)</span> are in reverse order with respect to those of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{i-1}\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>.</p>
                    </dd></dl>
              
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar6">Proof</h3>
                <p>The proof is by induction on <i>h</i>.</p>
                <p><b>Base case </b> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(h=0\)</span>. We describe how to define a 3UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma =\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H_{0,s}\)</span>. By Lemma <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar3">1</a>, every 3UBE of the subgraph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_0\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H_{0,s}\)</span> satisfies conditions <span class="u-sans-serif">S1</span>–<span class="u-sans-serif">S3</span>.</p>
                <p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{-1,j}\)</span> (with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(1 \le j \le s\)</span>) be a vertex of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{-1}\)</span>. The edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{-1},v_{-1,j})\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{-1,j},t_{-1})\)</span> imply <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (p_{-1}) < \pi (v_{-1,j})\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (v_{-1,j}) < \pi (t_{-1})\)</span>, which proves condition <span class="u-sans-serif">F1</span> for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{-1}\)</span>. Consider now the group <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _0\)</span>; the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{0},v_{0,j})\)</span> implies <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (p_{0}) < \pi (v_{0,j})\)</span>. On the other hand, if we put <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{0,j}\)</span> after <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_0\)</span>, the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{-1,j},v_{0,j})\)</span>, the channel edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{-1},t_0)\)</span>, and the two forcing edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_0,s'_0)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((q_0,q'_0)\)</span> would mutually conflict. But then a 3UBE would not exist by Property <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar2">1</a>. Thus, each vertex of group <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _0\)</span> must be between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_0\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_0\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>, which implies condition <span class="u-sans-serif">F1</span> for the group <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{0}\)</span>. As for the page assignment <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\sigma \)</span>, each <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{-1,j},v_{0,j})\)</span> conflicts with each forcing edge of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_0\)</span>, and thus it must be in the page of the channel edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_0\)</span>. This implies condition <span class="u-sans-serif">F2</span>. The edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{-1,j},v_{0,j})\)</span> can be assigned to the same page only if the vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{-1}\)</span> appear in reverse order with respect to those of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _0\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>. Thus, condition <span class="u-sans-serif">F3</span> holds and a 3UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H_{0,s}\)</span> can be defined by choosing an arbitrary order for the vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{-1}\)</span> and the reverse order for the vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _0\)</span>.</p>
                <p><b>Inductive case </b> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(h>0\)</span>. Consider the subgraph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H'_{h,s}\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H_{h,s}\)</span> consisting of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H_{h-2,s}\)</span> plus the exclusive vertices and edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{h-1}\)</span> and of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span>. By induction and by Lemma <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar3">1</a>, every 3UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma '=\langle \pi ', \sigma ' \rangle \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H'_{h,s}\)</span> satisfies conditions <span class="u-sans-serif">F1</span>–<span class="u-sans-serif">F3</span> and conditions <span class="u-sans-serif">S1</span>–<span class="u-sans-serif">S3</span>. We extend <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma '\)</span> to a 3UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma =\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H_{h,s}\)</span> as follows. By condition <span class="u-sans-serif">F1</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma '\)</span>, each vertex <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{h-2,j}\)</span> is before <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{h-2}\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi '\)</span>; on the other hand, because of the edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_h,v_{h,j})\)</span>, each vertex <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{h,j}\)</span> must follow <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_{h}\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>. This implies that each <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{h-1,j}\)</span> is between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_{h-1}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{h-1}\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>. Indeed, if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{h-1,j}\)</span> was before <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_{h-1}\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>, the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{h-1,j},v_{h,j})\)</span>, the channel edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{h-1},t_h)\)</span> and the two forcing edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span> would mutually conflict and therefore a 3UBE would not exist by Property <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar2">1</a>. On the other hand, if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{h-1,j}\)</span> was after <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{h-1}\)</span>, the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{h-2,j},v_{h-1,j})\)</span>, the channel edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{h-2},t_{h-1})\)</span> and the two forcing edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{h-1}\)</span> would mutually conflict and again a 3UBE would not exist by Property <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar2">1</a>. Thus, each vertex of group <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{h-1}\)</span> must be between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_{h-1}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{h-1}\)</span>, which proves condition <span class="u-sans-serif">F1</span> for the group <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{h-1}\)</span>. Consider now a vertex <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{h,j}\)</span>. If it was after <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_h\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>, then the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{h-1,j},v_{h,j})\)</span>, the channel edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((p_{h-1},t_{h})\)</span> and the two forcing edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span> would mutually conflict – again a 3UBE would not exist by Property <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar2">1</a>. Hence, each vertex of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _h\)</span> is between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_h\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_h\)</span>, which proves condition <span class="u-sans-serif">F1</span> also for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _h\)</span>.</p>
                <p>As for the page assignment <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\sigma \)</span>, each <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{h-2,j},v_{h-1,j})\)</span> conflicts with each forcing edge of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{h-1}\)</span> and hence it must be in the page of the channel edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{h-1}\)</span>. The same argument applies to the edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{h-1,j},v_{h,j})\)</span> with respect to the forcing edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span>. Thus the edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{h-1,j},v_{h,j})\)</span> must be in the page of the channel edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_h\)</span>, which proves condition <span class="u-sans-serif">F2</span>.</p>
                <p>The edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{h-2,j},v_{h-1,j})\)</span> can be assigned to the same page only if the vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{h-2}\)</span> appear in reverse order with respect to those of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{h-1}\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>. Similarly, the edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{h-1,j},v_{h,j})\)</span> can be assigned to the same page only if the vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{h-1}\)</span> appear in reverse order with respect to those of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{h}\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>. Thus, condition <span class="u-sans-serif">F3</span> holds and a 3UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H_{h,s}\)</span> can be defined by ordering the vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{h-1}\)</span> in reverse order with respect to those of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{h-2}\)</span> and the vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _h\)</span> with the same order as those of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{h-2}\)</span>. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
              <p>Observe that, by Condition <span class="u-sans-serif">F3</span>, all groups <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _i\)</span> with even index have the same ordering in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span> and all groups with odd index have the opposite order. As mentioned above the vertices in the groups <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _i\)</span> will correspond to the elements of the set <i>S</i> of an instance of <span class="u-small-caps">Betweenness</span> in the reduced instance of 3<span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span>. If the reduced instance admits a 3UBE, the order of the odd-indexed groups in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span> will give the desired order for the instance of <span class="u-small-caps">Betweenness</span>.</p><div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-5" data-title="Fig. 5"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig5" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 5</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" height="450"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-5-desc"><p><b>a</b> A gadget <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Lambda _i\)</span> data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/5" data-track-dest="link:Figure5 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 5" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div><p><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mathbf {\Lambda }\)</span>-<b>filled shell digraphs and hardness proof.</b> Starting from a filled shell digraph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H_{h,s}\)</span>, a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Lambda \)</span>-<i>filled shell digraph</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\widehat{H}}_{h,s}\)</span> is obtained by replacing some edges with a gadget that has two possible configurations in any 3UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\widehat{H}}_{h,s}\)</span>. More precisely, we replace each edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t'_i,p_i)\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H_{h,s}\)</span> for <i>i</i> odd with the gadget shown in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig5">5</a>a. The gadget replacing <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t'_i,p_i)\)</span> will be denoted as <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Lambda _i\)</span>. Notice that this replacement preserves Conditions <span class="u-sans-serif">F1</span>–<span class="u-sans-serif">F3</span> of Lemma <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar5">2</a>.</p>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar7">Lemma 3</h3>
                <p>Every <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Lambda \)</span>-filled shell digraph<span class="mathjax-tex">\({\widehat{H}}_{h,s}\)</span> for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s>0\)</span> and even <span class="mathjax-tex">\(h \ge 0\)</span> admits a 3UBE. In any 3UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma =\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\widehat{H}}_{h,s}\)</span> the following conditions hold for every odd <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=1,3,\dots ,h-1\)</span>: </p><dl class="c-abbreviation_list"><dt class="c-abbreviation_list__term u-text-bold u-float-left u-pr-16" style="min-width:50px;"><dfn><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textsf {G1}\)</span>:</dfn></dt><dd class="c-abbreviation_list__description u-mb-24">
                      <p>the vertices of the gadget <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Lambda _{i}\)</span> are between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'_{i}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_{i}\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>;</p>
                    </dd><dt class="c-abbreviation_list__term u-text-bold u-float-left u-pr-16" style="min-width:50px;"><dfn><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textsf {G2}\)</span>:</dfn></dt><dd class="c-abbreviation_list__description u-mb-24">
                      <p>the vertices <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x_{i}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y_{i}\)</span> are between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(w_{i}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z_{i}\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span> and there exists a 3UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma '=\langle \pi ', \sigma ' \rangle \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\widehat{H}}_{h,s}\)</span> where the order of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x_i\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y_i\)</span> is exchanged in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi '\)</span>.</p>
                    </dd></dl>
              
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar8">Proof</h3>
                <p>By Lemma <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar5">2</a>, every 3UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma '=\langle \pi ',\sigma '\rangle \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H_{h,s}\)</span> is such that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'_{i}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_{i}\)</span> are consecutive in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi '\)</span> for each <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=0,1,\dots ,h\)</span>. Notice that the gadget <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Lambda _{i}\)</span> admits a 3UBE (actually a 2UBE) <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma _{i}\)</span>. If we replace the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t'_{i},p_{i})\)</span> with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma _{i}\)</span>, we do not create any conflict between the edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Lambda _i\)</span> and the other edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(H_{h,s}\)</span>. This proves that <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\widehat{H}}_{h,s}\)</span> has a 3UBE.</p>
                <p>About condition <span class="u-sans-serif">G1</span>, observe that since any vertex of the gadget <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Lambda _{i}\)</span> belongs to a directed path from <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'_{i}\)</span> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_{i}\)</span>, then the vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Lambda _{i}\)</span> must be between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'_{i}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_{i}\)</span>. About condition <span class="u-sans-serif">G2</span>, observe that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x_{i}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y_{i}\)</span> both appear in a directed path from <span class="mathjax-tex">\(w_{i}\)</span> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z_{i}\)</span> and therefore they must be between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(w_i\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z_i\)</span>. Also, suppose that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (x_i) < \pi (y_i)\)</span> (the other case is symmetric). If we exchange the order of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x_i\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y_i\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi '\)</span> we introduce a conflict between <span class="mathjax-tex">\((w_i,x_i)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((y_i,z_i)\)</span>, which do not conflict with any other edges. If they are in the same page in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma \)</span> it is sufficient to change the page of one of them in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma '\)</span>. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
              <div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-6" data-title="Fig. 6"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig6" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 6</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/6" rel="nofollow"><picture><source type="image/webp" srcset="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig6_HTML.png?as=webp"><img aria-describedby="figure-6-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig6_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 6" loading="lazy" width="685" height="787"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-6-desc"><p><b>A</b> 3UBE of the <i>st</i>-graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_I\)</span> reduced from a positive instance <span class="mathjax-tex">\(I=\langle S,R\rangle \)</span> of <span class="u-small-caps">Betweenness</span>; the edge colors represent the corresponding pages</p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/6" data-track-dest="link:Figure6 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 6" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar9">Theorem 2</h3>
                <p>3<span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span> is NP-complete even for <i>st</i>-graphs.</p>
              
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar10">Proof</h3>
                <p>3<span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span> is clearly in NP. To prove the hardness we describe a reduction from <span class="u-small-caps">Betweenness</span>. From an instance <span class="mathjax-tex">\(I=\langle S, R \rangle \)</span> of <span class="u-small-caps">Betweenness</span> we construct an instance <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_I\)</span> of 3<span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span> that is an <i>st</i>-graph; we start from the <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Lambda \)</span>-filled shell digraph<span class="mathjax-tex">\({\widehat{H}}_{h,s}\)</span> with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(h=2 |R |\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s=|S |\)</span>. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_1,v_2,\dots ,v_s\)</span> be the elements of <i>S</i>. They are represented in <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\widehat{H}}_{h,s}\)</span> by the vertices <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{i,1},v_{i,2},\dots ,v_{i,s}\)</span> of the groups <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _i\)</span>, for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=-1,0,1,\dots ,h\)</span>. In the reduction each group <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _i\)</span> with odd index is used to encode one triplet and, in a 3UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_I\)</span>, the order of the vertices in these groups (which is the same by condition <span class="u-sans-serif">F3</span>) corresponds to the desired order of the elements of <i>S</i> for the instance <i>I</i>. Number the triplets of <i>R</i> from 1 to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(|R |\)</span> and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_a,v_b,v_c)\)</span> be the <i>j</i>-th triplet. We use the group <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{i}\)</span> and the gadget <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Lambda _{i}\)</span> with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=2j-1\)</span> to encode the triplet <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_a,v_b,v_c)\)</span>. More precisely, we add to <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\widehat{H}}_{h,s}\)</span> the edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((x_{i},v_{i,a})\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\((x_{i},v_{i,b})\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\((y_{i},v_{i,b})\)</span>, and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((y_{i},v_{i,c})\)</span> (see Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig5">5</a>b). These edges are called <i>triplet edges</i> and are denoted as <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_i\)</span>. We will show that in any 3UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_I\)</span> the triplet edges are forced to be in the same page and this is possible if and only if the constraints defined by the triplets in <i>R</i> are respected. The digraph obtained by the addition of the triplet edges is not an <i>st</i>-graph because the vertices of the last group <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{h}\)</span> are all sinks. The desired instance <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_I\)</span> of 3<span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span> is the <i>st</i>-graph obtained by adding the edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{h,j},t_h)\)</span> (for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(j=1,2,\dots ,s\)</span>). Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig6">6</a> shows a 3UBE of the <i>st</i>-graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_I\)</span> reduced from a positive instance <i>I</i> of <span class="u-small-caps">Betweenness</span>.</p>
                <p>We now show that <i>I</i> is a positive instance of <span class="u-small-caps">Betweenness</span> if and only if the <i>st</i>-graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_I\)</span> constructed as described above admits a 3UBE. Suppose first that <i>I</i> is a positive instance of <span class="u-small-caps">Betweenness</span>, i.e., there exists an ordering <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\tau \)</span> of <i>S</i> that satisfies all triplets in <i>R</i>. The subgraph <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\widehat{H}}_{h,s}\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_I\)</span> admits a 3UBE that satisfies conditions <span class="u-sans-serif">S1</span>–<span class="u-sans-serif">S3</span>, <span class="u-sans-serif">F1</span>–<span class="u-sans-serif">F3</span>, and <span class="u-sans-serif">G1</span>–<span class="u-sans-serif">G2</span> by Lemmas <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar3">1</a>,<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar5">2</a> and <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar7">3</a>. Observe that the order of the vertices of the groups <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _i\)</span> can be arbitrarily chosen (provided that all groups with even index have the same order and the groups with odd index have the reverse order). Thus we can choose the order of the groups with odd index to be equal to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\tau \)</span>. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma =\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span> be the resulting 3UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\widehat{H}}_{h,s}\)</span>. We now show that if we add the triplet edges to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma \)</span>, these edges do not conflict. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_a,v_b,v_c)\)</span> be the triplet encoded by the triplet edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_i\)</span> and suppose that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\tau (v_a)< \tau (v_b) < \tau (v_c)\)</span> (the other case is symmetric). Since the vertices of the groups with odd index are ordered in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span> as in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\tau \)</span>, we have <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (v_{i,a})< \pi (v_{i,b}) < \pi (v_{i,c})\)</span>.
