If <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (x_i) < \pi (y_i)\)</span> then the edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_i\)</span> do not conflict. If otherwise <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (y_i) < \pi (x_i)\)</span>, by condition <span class="u-sans-serif">G2</span> we can exchange the order of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x_i\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y_i\)</span>, thus guaranteeing again that the triplet edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_i\)</span> do not conflict. On the other hand, the triplet edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_i\)</span> conflict with the edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\(E^{\Lambda }_i\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Lambda _i\)</span>, with the channel edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\(E^{ch}_i\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_i\)</span>, and with all the edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\(E^{\alpha }_i\)</span> connecting group <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{i-1}\)</span> to group <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _{i}\)</span>. All the edges in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(E^{\Lambda }_i \cup E^{ch}_i \cup E^{\alpha }_i\)</span> can be assigned to only two pages. Indeed, the edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\(E^{\Lambda }_i\)</span> require two pages, while one page is enough for the edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(E^{ch}_i \cup E^{\alpha }_i\)</span>. Also, since the edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(E^{\Lambda }_i\)</span> do not conflict with those in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(E^{ch}_i \cup E^{\alpha }_i\)</span>, two pages suffice for all of them. Hence, the triplet edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_i\)</span> can all be assigned to the third page. Since this is true for all the triplet edges, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_I\)</span> admits a 3UBE.</p>
                <p>Suppose now that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_I\)</span> admits a 3UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma =\langle \pi ,\sigma \rangle \)</span>. By Lemmas <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar3">1</a>,<a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar5">2</a> and <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar7">3</a>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma \)</span> satisfies conditions <span class="u-sans-serif">S1</span>–<span class="u-sans-serif">S3</span>, <span class="u-sans-serif">F1</span>–<span class="u-sans-serif">F3</span>, and <span class="u-sans-serif">G1</span>–<span class="u-sans-serif">G2</span> . By condition <span class="u-sans-serif">F2</span> the order of the vertices of the groups <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha _i\)</span> with odd index is the same for all groups. We claim that all triplets in <i>R</i> are satisfied if this order is used as the order <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\tau \)</span> for the elements of <i>S</i>. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_a,v_b,v_c)\)</span> be the triplet encoded by the triplet edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_i\)</span>. By condition <span class="u-sans-serif">G2</span>, the vertices <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x_i\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y_i\)</span> of the gadget <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Lambda _i\)</span> are between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(w_i\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z_i\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span>. Thus the triplet edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_i\)</span> conflict with the edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_i,z_i)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((w_i,p_i)\)</span>. These two edges must be in two different pages because they conflict. It follows that the triplet edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_i\)</span> must all be in the same page, i.e., the third one. Since the three edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_i\)</span> are in the same page we have either <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (x_i) < \pi (y_i)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (v_{i,a})< \pi (v_{i,b}) < \pi (v_{i,c})\)</span> or <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (y_i) < \pi (x_i)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (v_{i,c})< \pi (v_{i,b}) < \pi (v_{i,a})\)</span> (any other order would cause a crossing between the edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_i\)</span>). In both cases vertex <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{i,b}\)</span> is between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{i,a}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{i,c}\)</span>, i.e., <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_b\)</span> is between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_a\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_c\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\tau \)</span>. Since this is true for all triplets, <i>I</i> is a positive instance of <span class="u-small-caps">Betweenness</span>. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
              <p>For <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k>3\)</span>, the reduction from an instance <i>I</i> of <span class="u-small-caps">Betweenness</span> to an instance <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_I\)</span> of <i>k</i> <span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span> is similar. In the shell digraph every pair of forcing edges is replaced by a bundle of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k-1\)</span> edges that mutually conflict (see Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig7">7</a>a). The edges in each bundle require <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k-1\)</span> pages and force all edges that conflict with them to use the <i>k</i>-th page. Analogously, the two edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_i,z_i)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((w_i,p_i)\)</span> of the gadget <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Lambda _i\)</span> are replaced by a bundle of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k-1\)</span> edges that mutually conflict (see Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig7">7</a>b); this forces the triplet edges to be in the <i>k</i>-th page. Finally, observe that our reduction does not work for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k=2\)</span>, as it would yield an instance of 2UBE that never admits a book embedding on two pages, i.e., it is always a negative instance.</p>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar11">Corollary 1</h3>
                <p><i>k</i> <span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span> is NP-complete for every <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k \ge 3\)</span>, even for <i>st</i>-graphs.</p>
              <div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-7" data-title="Fig. 7"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig7" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 7</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/7" rel="nofollow"><picture><source type="image/webp" srcset="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig7_HTML.png?as=webp"><img aria-describedby="figure-7-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig7_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 7" loading="lazy" width="685" height="465"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-7-desc"><p>Reduction for <i>k</i> <span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span> (example with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k=5\)</span>). <b>a</b> Replacement of the forcing edges. <b>b</b> Replacement of the gadget <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Lambda _i\)</span>. In both figures colors represent the pages</p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/7" data-track-dest="link:Figure7 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 7" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div></div></div></section><section data-title="Existential Results for 2UBE"><div class="c-article-section" id="Sec4-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="Sec4"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">4 </span>Existential Results for 2UBE</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="Sec4-content"><p>Let <i>f</i> be an internal face of a plane <i>st</i>-graph, and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_l\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_r\)</span> be the left and the right path of <i>f</i>; <i>f</i> is a <i>generalized triangle</i> if either <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_l\)</span> or <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_r\)</span> is a single edge (i.e., a transitive edge), and it is a <i>rhombus</i> if each of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_l\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_r\)</span> consists of exactly two edges (see Figs. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig8">8</a>a and <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig8">8</a>b).</p><p>Let <i>G</i> be a plane <i>st</i>-graph. A <i>forbidden configuration</i> of <i>G</i> consists of a transitive edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\(e=(u,v)\)</span> shared by two internal faces <i>f</i> and <i>g</i> such that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_f=s_g=u\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_f=t_g=v\)</span> (i.e., two generalized triangles sharing the transitive edge); see Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig8">8</a>c. The absence of forbidden configurations is necessary for the existence of an embedding-preserving <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE. If <i>G</i> is triangulated, the absence of forbidden configurations is also a sufficient condition [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 81" title="Mchedlidze, T., Symvonis, A.: Crossing-optimal acyclic HP-completion for outerplanar st-digraphs. J. Graph Algorithms and Appl. 15(3), 373–415 (2011)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR81" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e20588">81</a>].</p><div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-8" data-title="Fig. 8"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig8" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 8</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/8" rel="nofollow"><picture><img aria-describedby="figure-8-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig8_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 8" loading="lazy" width="685" height="361"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-8-desc"><p><b>a</b> A generalized triangle <i>G</i>. <b>b</b> A rhombus <b>c</b> A forbidden configuration</p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/8" data-track-dest="link:Figure8 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 8" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar12">Theorem 3</h3>
                <p>Any plane <i>st</i>-graph such that the left and the right path of every internal face contain at least two and three edges, respectively, admits an embedding-preserving <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE.</p>
              
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar13">Proof</h3>
                <p>Let <i>G</i> be a plane <i>st</i>-graph such that the left and the right path of every internal face contain at least two and three edges, respectively. We prove how to construct an embedding preserving HP-completion. The idea is to construct <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}\)</span> by adding a face of <i>G</i> per time from left to right. Namely, the faces of <i>G</i> are added according to a topological ordering of the dual graph of <i>G</i>. When a face <i>f</i> is added, its right path is attached to the right boundary of the current digraph. We maintain the invariant that at least one edge <i>e</i> in the left path of <i>f</i> belongs to the Hamiltonian path of the current digraph. The Hamiltonian path is extended by replacing <i>e</i> with a path that traverses the vertices of the right path of <i>f</i>. To this aim, dummy edges are suitably inserted inside <i>f</i>. When all faces are added, the resulting graph is an HP-completion <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}\)</span> of <i>G</i>.</p>
                <p>More precisely, let <i>N</i> be the number of internal faces of <i>G</i>, and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G^*\)</span> be the dual graph of <i>G</i>. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s^*=f_0,f_1,\dots ,f_N,f_{N+1}=t^*\)</span> be a topological ordering of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G^*\)</span>. Denote by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_0\)</span> the left boundary of <i>G</i> and by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_i\)</span>, for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=1,2,\dots ,N\)</span>, the subgraph of <i>G</i> consisting of the faces <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_1,f_2,\dots ,f_i\)</span>. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_i\)</span> can be obtained by adding the right path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p^i_r\)</span> of face <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_i\)</span> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{i-1}\)</span>. We construct a sequence <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_0, {\overline{G}}_1,\dots , {\overline{G}}_N\)</span> of <i>st</i>-graphs such that <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_i\)</span> is an HP-completion of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_i\)</span>. Clearly, <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_N\)</span> will be an HP-completion of <i>G</i>. While constructing the sequence, we maintain the following invariant: given any two consecutive edges along the right boundary of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_i\)</span>, at least one of them belongs to the Hamiltonian path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_i}\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_i\)</span>. <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_0\)</span> coincides with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_0\)</span> and all its edges are in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_0}\)</span>, so the invariant holds. Suppose then that <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_{i-1}\)</span>, with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i>1\)</span>, satisfies the invariant. To construct <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_i\)</span> we must add the right path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p^i_r\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_i\)</span> plus possibly some dummy edges inside <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_i\)</span>. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_f=v_0,v_1,v_2,\dots ,v_{k-1},t_f=v_k\)</span> be the right path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p^i_r\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_i\)</span> and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_f=u_0,u_1,u_2,\dots ,u_{h-1},t_f=u_h\)</span> be the left path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p^i_l\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_i\)</span>. By hypothesis <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p^i_r\)</span> has at least three edges, and therefore <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k \ge 3\)</span>; moreover, since <i>G</i> has no transitive edge, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(h\ge 2\)</span>. Notice that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p^i_l\)</span> is a subpath of the right boundary of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_{i-1}\)</span> and that the right boundary of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_i\)</span> is obtained from the right boundary of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_{i-1}\)</span> by replacing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p^i_l\)</span> with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p^i_r\)</span>. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_{-1},s_f)\)</span> be the edge along the right boundary of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_{i-1}\)</span> entering <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_f\)</span> and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t_f,u_{h+1})\)</span> be the edge along the right boundary of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_{i-1}\)</span> exiting <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_f\)</span>; each of these edges may not exist if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_f=s\)</span> or <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_f=t\)</span>. We have different cases depending on whether <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,u_1)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_{h-1},t_f)\)</span> belong to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i-1}}\)</span> or not.</p><div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-9" data-title="Fig. 9"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig9" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 9</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/9" rel="nofollow"><picture><source type="image/webp" srcset="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig9_HTML.png?as=webp"><img aria-describedby="figure-9-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig9_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 9" loading="lazy" width="685" height="687"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-9-desc"><p>Illustration for Theorem <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar12">3</a>: <b>a</b>, <b>b</b> Case 1; <b>c</b>, <b>d</b> Case 2; <b>e</b>, <b>f</b> Case 4</p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/9" data-track-dest="link:Figure9 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 9" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div>
                <p><b>Case 1: neither </b><span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,u_1)\)</span> <b>nor </b><span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_{h-1},t_f)\)</span> <b>belong to </b> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i-1}}\)</span>. See Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig9">9</a> for an illustration. By the invariant there is an edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_j,u_{j+1})\)</span> with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(0 \le j \le h-1\)</span> between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_f\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_f\)</span> that belongs to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i-1}}\)</span>. We add the two dummy edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_j,v_1)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{k-1},u_{j+1})\)</span>, thus “extending” <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i-1}}\)</span> to a Hamiltonian path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i}}\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_i\)</span>; namely, the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_j,u_{j+1})\)</span> is bypassed by the path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u_j,v_1,v_2,\dots ,v_{k-1},u_{j+1}\)</span>. The only edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p^i_r\)</span> that do not belong to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i}}\)</span> are <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,v_1)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{k-1},t_f)\)</span>. If <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_{-1},s_f)\)</span> exists, then by the invariant it belongs to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i-1}}\)</span> (because <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,u_1)\)</span> does not) and thus the invariant is preserved for <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,v_1)\)</span> and its preceding and following edges. Analogously, if <span class="mathjax-tex">\((t_f,u_{h+1})\)</span> exists, then by the invariant it belongs to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i-1}}\)</span> and thus the invariant is preserved for <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{k-1},t_f)\)</span> and its preceding and following edges.</p>
                <p><b>Case 2: </b><span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,u_{1})\)</span> <b>does not belong to</b> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i-1}}\)</span>,<b>while </b><span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_{h-1},t_f)\)</span><b>does.</b> See Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig9">9</a> for an illustration. We add the dummy edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_{h-1},v_1)\)</span>. This“extends” <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i-1}}\)</span> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i}}\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_i\)</span> bypassing the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_{h-1},t_f)\)</span> with the path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u_{h-1},v_1,v_2,\dots ,v_{k-1},t_f\)</span>. The only edge of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p^i_r\)</span> that does not belong to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i}}\)</span> is <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,v_1)\)</span>. If <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_{-1},s_f)\)</span> exists, then by the invariant it belongs to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i-1}}\)</span> (because <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,u_1)\)</span> does not) and thus the invariant is preserved for <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,v_1)\)</span> and its preceding and following edges.</p>
                <p><b>Case 3: </b><span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,u_{1})\)</span> <b>belongs to</b> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i-1}}\)</span>, <b>while</b> <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_{h-1},t_f)\)</span> <b>does not.</b> This case is symmetric to the previous one.</p>
                <p><b>Case 4: both </b><span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,u_1)\)</span><b>and </b><span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_{h-1},t_f)\)</span> <b>belong to </b> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i-1}}\)</span>. See Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig9">9</a> for an illustration. We add the two dummy edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{k-2},u_1)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_{h-1},v_{k-1})\)</span>. In this case we “extend” <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i-1}}\)</span> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i}}\)</span> bypassing <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,u_{1})\)</span> with the path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_f,v_1,\dots ,v_{k-2}\)</span> and bypassing the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_{h-1},t_f)\)</span> with the path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u_{h-1}, v_{k-1}, t_f\)</span>. The only edge of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p^i_r\)</span> that does not belong to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i}}\)</span> is <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{k-2},v_{k-1})\)</span>; moreover, all the edges <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,v_1), (v_1,v_2), \dots , (v_{k-3},v_{k-2})\)</span> and the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_{k-1},t_f)\)</span> belong to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i}}\)</span>. Thus the invariant is preserved. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
              <p>The next theorem is proved with a construction similar to that of Theorem <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar12">3</a>.</p>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar14">Theorem 4</h3>
                <p>Let <i>G</i> be a plane <i>st</i>-graph such that every internal face of <i>G</i> is a rhombus. Then <i>G</i> admits an embedding-preserving <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE.</p>
              
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar15">Proof</h3>
                <p>During the construction of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_i\)</span> from <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_{i-1}\)</span>, we maintain the same invariant as in Theorem <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar12">3</a>. If all faces are rhombi, when we construct <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_i\)</span> from <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\overline{G}}_{i-1}\)</span>, we have that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p^i_l\)</span> is a path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_f,u_1,t_f\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p^i_r\)</span> is a path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_f,v_1,t_f\)</span>. At least one of <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,u_1)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_1,t_f)\)</span> belongs to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i-1}}\)</span>. If <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,u_1)\)</span> belongs to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i-1}}\)</span> we add the dummy edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((v_1,u_1)\)</span>. In this case we bypass the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_f,u_1)\)</span> with the path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_f,v_1,u_1\)</span>. If <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_1,t_f)\)</span> belongs to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(P_{{\overline{G}}_{i-1}}\)</span> we add the dummy edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_1,v_1)\)</span>. In this case we bypass the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u_1,t_f)\)</span> with the path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u_1,v_1,t_f\)</span>. In both cases it is easy to see that the invariant is maintained. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
              </div></div></section><section data-title="Testing 2UBE for Plane Graphs with Special Faces"><div class="c-article-section" id="Sec5-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="Sec5"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">5 </span>Testing 2UBE for Plane Graphs with Special Faces</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="Sec5-content"><p>By Theorem <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar12">3</a>, if all internal faces of a plane <i>st</i>-graph <i>G</i> are such that their left and right path contain at least two and at least three edges, respectively, <i>G</i> admits an embedding-preserving <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE. If these conditions do not hold, an embedding-preserving <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE may not exist (see Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig10">10</a>a). We now describe an efficient testing algorithm for a plane <i>st</i>-graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G=(V,E)\)</span> whose internal faces are generalized triangles or rhombi (see Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig10">10</a>b). We construct a <i>mixed</i> graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_M=(V,E \cup E_U)\)</span>, where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(E_U\)</span> is a set of undirected edges and <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u,v) \in E_U\)</span> if <i>u</i> and <i>v</i> are the two vertices of a rhombus face <i>f</i> distinct from <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_f\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_f\)</span> (blue edges in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig10">10</a>c). For a rhombus face <i>f</i>, the graph obtained from <i>G</i> by adding the directed edge (<i>u</i>, <i>v</i>) inside <i>f</i> is still a plane <i>st</i>-graph (see, e.g. [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 22" title="Bekos, M.A., Di Giacomo, E., Didimo, W., Liotta, G., Montecchiani, F., Raftopoulou, C.N.: Edge partitions of optimal 2-plane and 3-plane graphs. In: Brandstädt, A., Köhler, E., Meer, K., (eds.) Graph-Theoretic Concepts in Computer Science, WG 2018, volume 11159 of LNCS, pages 27–39. Springer, (2018)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR22" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e26404">22</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 41" title="Di Battista, G., Eades, P., Tamassia, R., Tollis, I.G.: Graph Drawing: Algorithms for the Visualization of Graphs. Prentice Hall PTR, USA (1998)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR41" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e26407">41</a>]). Since there is only one edge of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(E_U\)</span> inside each rhombus face of <i>G</i>, the following holds.</p>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar16">Property 2</h3>
                <p>Every orientation of the edges in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(E_U\)</span> transforms <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_M\)</span> into an acyclic digraph.</p>
              <div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-10" data-title="Fig. 10"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig10" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 10</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/10" rel="nofollow"><picture><source type="image/webp" srcset="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig10_HTML.png?as=webp"><img aria-describedby="figure-10-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig10_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 10" loading="lazy" width="685" height="244"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-10-desc"><p><b>a</b> A plane <i>st</i>-graph that does not admit a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 82" title="Nowakowski, R., Parker, A.: Ordered sets, pagenumbers and planarity. Order 6(3), 209–218 (1989)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR82" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e26525">82</a>]. <b>b</b> A plane <i>st</i>-graph <i>G</i> whose faces are generalized triangles or rhombi. <b>b</b> The mixed graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_M=(V,E,E_U)\)</span></p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/10" data-track-dest="link:Figure10 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 10" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar17">Theorem 5</h3>
                <p>Let <i>G</i> be a plane <i>st</i>-graph such that every internal face of <i>G</i> is either a generalized triangle or a rhombus. There is an <i>O</i>(<i>n</i>)-time algorithm that decides whether <i>G</i> admits an embedding-preserving <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE, and which computes it in the positive case.</p>
              
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar18">Proof</h3>
                <p>The edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(E_U\)</span> are the only edges that can be used to construct an embedding-preserving HP-completion of <i>G</i>. This, together with Theorem <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar1">1</a>, implies that <i>G</i> admits a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE if and only if the undirected edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_M\)</span> can be oriented so that the resulting digraph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\overrightarrow{G_M}\)</span> has a directed Hamiltonian path from <i>s</i> to <i>t</i>. By Property <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar16">2</a>, any orientation of the undirected edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_M\)</span> gives rise to an acyclic digraph. On the other hand an acyclic digraph is Hamiltonian if and only if it is unilateral (see, e.g. [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 6" title="Angelini, P., Bekos, M.A., Didimo, W., Grilli, L., Kindermann, P., Mchedlidze, T., Prutkin, R., Symvonis, A., Tappini, A.: Greedy rectilinear drawings. In: Biedl, T., Kerren, A. (eds.) GD 2018, volume 11282 of LNCS. Springer, (2018)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR6" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e26789">6</a>, Theorem 4]); we recall that a digraph is <i>unilateral</i> if each pair of vertices is connected by a directed path (in at least one of the two directions) [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 80" title="Mchedlidze, T., Symvonis, A.: Unilateral orientation of mixed graphs. In: SOFSEM 2010, volume 5901 of LNCS, pages 588–599. Springer, (2010)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR80" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e26795">80</a>]. Testing whether the undirected edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_M\)</span> can be oriented so that the resulting digraph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\overrightarrow{G_M}\)</span> is unilateral, and computing such an orientation if it exists, can be done in time <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(|V |+ |E |+ |E_U |) = O(n)\)</span> [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 80" title="Mchedlidze, T., Symvonis, A.: Unilateral orientation of mixed graphs. In: SOFSEM 2010, volume 5901 of LNCS, pages 588–599. Springer, (2010)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR80" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e26925">80</a>, Theorem 4]. A Hamiltonian path of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\overrightarrow{G_M}\)</span> is given by a topological ordering of its vertices. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
              </div></div></section><section data-title="Testing Algorithms for 2UBE Parameterized by the Branchwidth"><div class="c-article-section" id="Sec6-section"><h2 class="c-article-section__title js-section-title js-c-reading-companion-sections-item" id="Sec6"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">6 </span>Testing Algorithms for 2UBE Parameterized by the Branchwidth</h2><div class="c-article-section__content" id="Sec6-content"><p>In this section, we show that the 2<span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span> problem is fixed-parameter tractable with respect to the branchwidth of the input <i>st</i>-graph both in the fixed and in the variable embedding setting. Since the treewidth <i>tw</i>(<i>G</i>) and the branchwidth <i>bw</i>(<i>G</i>) of a graph <i>G</i> are within a constant factor from each other (i.e., <span class="mathjax-tex">\(bw(G) - 1 \le tw(G) \le \lfloor \frac{3}{2}bw(G) \rfloor -1\)</span> [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 86" title="Robertson, N., Seymour, P.D.: Graph minors. x. obstructions to tree-decomposition. J. Comb. Theory, Ser. B, 52(2):153–190,( 1991)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR86" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e27094">86</a>]), our FPT algorithm also extends to graphs of bounded treewidth.</p><p>We use the SPQR-tree data structure [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 43" title="Di Battista, G., Tamassia, R.: On-line planarity testing. SIAM J. Comput. 25(5), 956–997 (1996)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR43" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e27100">43</a>] to efficiently handle the planar embeddings of the input digraphs, and sphere-cut decompositions [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 87" title="Seymour, P.D., Thomas, R.: Call routing and the ratcatcher. Combinatorica 14(2), 217–241 (1994)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR87" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e27103">87</a>] to develop a dynamic-programming approach on the skeletons of the rigid components.</p><p>Before presenting the testing algorithm (Subsection <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Sec9">6.3</a>), we briefly recall the definition of the <i>SPQR-tree</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span> of a biconnected graph (Subsection <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Sec7">6.1</a>) and the definitions of <i>branchwidth</i> and <i>sphere-cut decomposition</i> (Subsection <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Sec8">6.2</a>).</p><h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec7"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">6.1 </span>SPQR-trees of Planar <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\textbf{st}\)</span>-Graphs</h3><div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-11" data-title="Fig. 11"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig11" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 11</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/11" rel="nofollow"><picture><source type="image/webp" srcset="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig11_HTML.png?as=webp"><img aria-describedby="figure-11-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig11_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 11" loading="lazy" width="685" height="446"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-11-desc"><p><b>a</b> A biconnected planar <i>st</i>-graph <i>G</i>, where <i>s</i> and <i>t</i> are labeled 1 and 14, respectively. <b>b</b> The SPQR-tree of <i>G</i> with respect to the edge (<i>s</i>, <i>t</i>); for each S-, P-, or R-node of the tree, the figure depicts the skeleton of the node. In <b>a</b>, the left and the right outer face of the S-node with poles 8 and 13 are yellow and green, respectively</p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/11" data-track-dest="link:Figure11 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 11" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div><p>Let <i>G</i> be a biconnected graph. An <i>SPQR-tree</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span> of <i>G</i> is a tree-like data structure that represents the decomposition of <i>G</i> into its triconnected components, which can be computed in linear time [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 43" title="Di Battista, G., Tamassia, R.: On-line planarity testing. SIAM J. Comput. 25(5), 956–997 (1996)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR43" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e27253">43</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 65" title="Gutwenger, C., Mutzel, P.: A linear time implementation of SPQR-trees. In: Marks, J. (ed) Graph Drawing, GD 2000, volume 1984 of LNCS, pages 77–90. Springer, (2000)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR65" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e27256">65</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 71" title="Hopcroft, J.E., Tarjan, R.E.: Dividing a graph into triconnected components. SIAM J. Comput. 2(3), 135–158 (1973)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR71" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e27259">71</a>]. See Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig11">11</a> for an illustration. Each node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span> of degree greater than one corresponds to a triconnected component of <i>G</i> (as defined in [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 71" title="Hopcroft, J.E., Tarjan, R.E.: Dividing a graph into triconnected components. SIAM J. Comput. 2(3), 135–158 (1973)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR71" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e27304">71</a>]) and has two special vertices, called <i>poles</i>. Namely, let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> be a node of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span> of degree greater than one, and let <i>u</i> and <i>v</i> be its poles; <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> is associated with a multigraph <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span>, called the <i>skeleton</i> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span>. The multigraph <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}^+(\mu )\)</span> obtained by adding an edge (<i>u</i>, <i>v</i>) to <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span> describes the triconnected component corresponding to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span>. Node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> is either an <i>R-node</i>, an <i>S-node</i>, or a <i>P-node</i>, based on the following cases:</p><ul class="u-list-style-dash">
                  <li>
                    <p>R-node: If <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}^+(\mu )\)</span> is triconnected;</p>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                    <p>S-node: If <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}^+(\mu )\)</span> is a cycle of length at least three;</p>
                  </li>
                  <li>
                    <p>P-node: If <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}^+(\mu )\)</span> is a bundle of at least three parallel edges;</p>
                  </li>
                </ul><p>Also, for each edge of <i>G</i>, there is a corresponding node in <i>T</i> having degree one, which is called a <i>Q-node</i>. If <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> is a Q-node that corresponds to an edge <i>e</i>, the <i>poles</i> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> coincide with the end-vertices of <i>e</i>, and <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span> coincides with <i>e</i>.</p><p>A <i>virtual edge</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(e_\nu \)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span> corresponds to a tree node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\nu \)</span> adjacent to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span>. In the following, we will regard <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\mathcal {T}}}\)</span> as rooted at an arbitrary Q-node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\rho \)</span>. The edge <i>e</i> of <i>G</i> corresponding to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\rho \)</span> is the <i>reference edge</i> of <i>G</i>, and <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span> is the SPQR-tree of <i>G</i> <i>with respect to</i> <i>e</i>. For every node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span>, the edges corresponding to the leaves (i.e., the Q-nodes) of the subtree <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}_\mu \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span> rooted at <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> induce a subgraph <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> of <i>G</i>, called the <i>pertinent graph</i> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span>. The graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> is described by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}_\mu \)</span> in the decomposition.</p><p>If <i>G</i> is planar, the SPQR-tree <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span> of <i>G</i> with respect to <i>e</i> implicitly describes all planar embeddings of <i>G</i> in which <i>e</i> is incident to the outer face. All such embeddings are obtained by combining the different planar embeddings of the skeletons of P- and R-nodes: For a P-node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span>, the different embeddings of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span> are the different permutations of its edges. If <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> is an R-node, <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span> has two possible planar embeddings, obtained by flipping <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span> at its poles. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> be a node of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span>, let <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {E}}\)</span> be an embedding of <i>G</i>, let <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {E}}_\mu \)</span> be the embedding <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {E}}\)</span>, and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f^{O}_\mu \)</span> be the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {E}}_\mu \)</span>. The path along <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f^{O}_\mu \)</span> between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span> that leaves <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f^{O}_\mu \)</span> to its left (resp. to its right) when traversing the boundary of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f^{O}_\mu \)</span> from <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span> is the <i>left outer path</i> (resp. the <i>right outer path</i>) of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {E}}_\mu \)</span>. The <i>left</i> (resp., <i>right</i>) <i>outer face</i> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {E}}_\mu \)</span> is the face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {E}}\)</span>, not belonging to <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {E}}_\mu \)</span>, that is incident to the left (resp., to the right) outer path of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {E}}_\mu \)</span>. In Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig11">11</a>a, the left and the right outer face of the S-node with poles 8 and 13 are yellow and green, respectively.</p><p>To ease the description, we can assume that each S-node has exactly two children [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 51" title="Didimo, W., Giordano, F., Liotta, G.: Upward spirality and upward planarity testing. SIAM J. Dis. Math. 23(4), 1842–1899 (2009)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR51" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e28878">51</a>]. In particular, we will exploit the following property of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span> when <i>G</i> is an <i>st</i>-graph containing the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\(e=(s,t)\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span> is rooted at the Q-node of <i>e</i>.</p>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar19">Property 3</h3>
                  <p>[<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 43" title="Di Battista, G., Tamassia, R.: On-line planarity testing. SIAM J. Comput. 25(5), 956–997 (1996)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR43" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e28969">43</a>] Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> be a node of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span> with poles <i>u</i> and <i>v</i>. We have that <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> is either a <i>uv</i>-graph or a <i>vu</i>-graph.</p>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec8"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">6.2 </span>Branch decomposition and sphere-cut decomposition</h3><p>A <i>branch decomposition</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle T,\xi \rangle \)</span> of a graph <i>G</i> consists of an unrooted binary tree <i>T</i> (i.e., each internal node of <i>T</i> has degree three) and of a bijection <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\xi : {\mathcal {L}}(T) \leftrightarrow E(G)\)</span> between the leaf set <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {L}}(T)\)</span> of <i>T</i> and the edge set <i>E</i>(<i>G</i>) of <i>G</i>. For each arc <i>a</i> of <i>T</i>, let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^a_1\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^a_2\)</span> be the two connected components of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T-a\)</span>, and, for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=1,2\)</span>, let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G^a_i\)</span> be the subgraph of <i>G</i> that consists of the edges corresponding to the leaves of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T^a_i\)</span>, i.e., the edge set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\{\xi (\mu ):\mu \in {\mathcal {L}}(T) \cap V(T^a_i)\}\)</span>. The <i>middle set</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{mid}\,}}(a) \subseteq V (G)\)</span> is the intersection of the vertex sets of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G^a_1\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G^a_2\)</span>, i.e., <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{mid}\,}}(a) := V(G^a_1) \cap V(G^a_2)\)</span>. The <i>width</i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\mathrm{\beta }\,}}(\langle T,\xi \rangle )\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle T,\xi \rangle \)</span> is the maximum size of the middle sets over all arcs of <i>T</i>, i.e., <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\mathrm{\beta }\,}}(\langle T,\xi \rangle ) := \max \{|{{\,\textrm{mid}\,}}(a) |: a \in E(T)\}\)</span>. An <i>optimal branch decomposition</i> of <i>G</i> is a branch decomposition with minimum width; this width is called the <i>branchwidth</i> of <i>G</i> and is denoted by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\mathrm{\beta }\,}}= bw(G)\)</span>.</p><p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Sigma \)</span> be a sphere. A <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Sigma \)</span>-<i>plane</i> graph <i>G</i> is a planar graph <i>G</i> embedded (i.e., topologically drawn) on <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Sigma \)</span>. A <i>noose</i> of a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Sigma \)</span>-plane graph <i>G</i> is a closed simple curve on <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Sigma \)</span> that (i) intersects <i>G</i> only at vertices and (ii) traverses each face at most once.</p><p>The <i>length</i> of a noose <i>O</i> is the number of vertices it intersects. Every noose <i>O</i> bounds two closed discs <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Delta ^1_O\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Delta ^2_O\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Sigma \)</span>, i.e., <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Delta ^1_O \cap \Delta ^2_O= O\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Delta ^1_O \cup \Delta ^2_O=\Sigma \)</span>.</p><p>Let <i>G</i> be a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Sigma \)</span>-plane graph and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle T,\xi \rangle \)</span> be a branch decomposition of <i>G</i>. Suppose that, for each arc <i>a</i> of <i>T</i>, there exists a noose <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O_a\)</span> that traverses all and only the vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{mid}\,}}(a)\)</span> and whose closed discs <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Delta ^1_{O_a}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Delta ^2_{O_a}\)</span> enclose the drawing of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G^a_1\)</span> and of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G^a_2\)</span>, respectively. Denote by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _a\)</span> the circular order of the vertices in <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{mid}\,}}(a)\)</span> as they appear along <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O_a\)</span> and by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Pi =\{\pi _a: a \in E(T)\}\)</span> the set of all the circular orders <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _a\)</span>. The triple <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle T,\xi ,\Pi \rangle \)</span> is a <i>sphere-cut decomposition</i> of <i>G</i>. A clockwise traversal of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O_a\)</span> in the drawing of <i>G</i> defines the cyclic ordering <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _a\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{mid}\,}}(a)\)</span>. We always assume that the vertices of each middle set <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{mid}\,}}(a) = V(G^a_1) \cap V(G^a_2)\)</span> are enumerated according to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _a\)</span>. Observe that, Condition II of the definition of noose implies that, for any arc <span class="mathjax-tex">\(a \in E(T)\)</span>, the graphs <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G^a_1\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G^a_2\)</span> are both connected. Also, the nooses are <i>pairwise non-crossing</i>, i.e., for any two nooses <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O_a\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O_b\)</span>, it holds that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O_b\)</span> lies either entirely inside <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Delta ^1_{O_a}\)</span> or entirely inside <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Delta ^2_{O_a}\)</span>. We will exploit the following result by Dorn et al [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 52" title="Dorn, F., Penninkx, E., Bodlaender, H.L., Fomin, F.V.: Efficient exact algorithms on planar graphs: exploiting sphere cut decompositions. Algorithmica 58(3), 790–810 (2010)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR52" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e30972">52</a>, Theorem 1].</p>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar20">Theorem 6</h3>
                  <p>[<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 52" title="Dorn, F., Penninkx, E., Bodlaender, H.L., Fomin, F.V.: Efficient exact algorithms on planar graphs: exploiting sphere cut decompositions. Algorithmica 58(3), 790–810 (2010)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR52" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e30983">52</a>] Let <i>G</i> be a connected <i>n</i>-vertex <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Sigma \)</span>-plane graph having branchwidth <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\mathrm{\beta }\,}}\)</span> and no vertex of degree one. There exists a sphere-cut decomposition of <i>G</i> having width <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\mathrm{\beta }\,}}\)</span> which can be constructed in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(n^3)\)</span> time.</p>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec9"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">6.3 </span>Algorithm description</h3><p>Without loss of generality, we assume that the input <i>st</i>-graph <i>G</i> contains the edge (<i>s</i>, <i>t</i>), which guarantees that <i>G</i> is biconnected. In fact, in any <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <i>G</i> vertices <i>s</i> and <i>t</i> have to be the first and the last vertex of the spine, respectively. Thus, either (<i>s</i>, <i>t</i>) is an edge of <i>G</i> or it can be added to any of the two pages of a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <i>G</i> to obtain a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi ,\sigma \rangle \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G \cup (s,t)\)</span>. Clearly, the edge (<i>s</i>, <i>t</i>) will be incident to the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi ,\sigma )\)</span>.</p><p><b>Overview.</b> Our approach builds upon a classification of the embeddings of each triconnected component of the biconnected graph <i>G</i>. Intuitively, such a classification is based on the visibility of the spine that the embedding “leaves” on its outer face. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\mathcal {T}}}\)</span> be the SPQR-tree of <i>G</i> with respect to the reference edge (<i>s</i>, <i>t</i>). For any node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\mathcal {T}}}\)</span>, we show that the planar embeddings of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> that yield a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> can be partitioned into a finite number of equivalence classes, called <i>embedding types</i>. By visiting <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span> bottom-up, we describe how to compute all the <i>realizable</i> embedding types of the pertinent graph of each node, that is, those embedding types that are allowed by some <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of it. To this aim we will exploit the realizable embedding types of the pertinent graphs of its children. If there exists at least one embedding type that is realizable by the pertinent graph of the unique child of the root of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span>, which coincides with the whole graph <i>G</i> minus the edge (<i>s</i>, <i>t</i>), then <i>G</i> admits a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE. The most challenging part of this approach is handling the triconnected components that correspond to the P-nodes, where the problem is reduced to a maximum flow problem on a capacitated flow network with edge demands, and to the R-nodes, where a sphere-cut decomposition of bounded width is used to efficiently compute the feasible embedding types.</p><p><b>Embedding Types</b> Given a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span>, the two pages will be called the <i>left page</i> (the one to the left of the spine) and the <i>right page</i> (the one to the right of the spine), respectively. We write <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\sigma (e)=L\)</span> (resp. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\sigma (e)=R\)</span>) if the edge <i>e</i> is assigned to the left page (resp. to the right page). A point <i>p</i> of the spine is <i>visible from the left (right) page</i> if it is possible to shoot a horizontal ray originating from <i>p</i> and directed leftward (rightward) without intersecting any edge in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi , \sigma )\)</span>.</p><p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> be a node of the SPQR-tree <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\mathcal {T}}}\)</span> of <i>G</i> rooted at (<i>s</i>, <i>t</i>). Recall that, by Property <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar19">3</a>, since <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\mathcal {T}}}\)</span> has been rooted at (<i>s</i>, <i>t</i>), the pertinent graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> and the skeleton <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> are <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s't'\)</span>-graphs, where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s'\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'\)</span> are the poles of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> . We denote by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span> (<span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span>) the pole of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> that is the source (resp. the sink) of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> and of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span>. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu \rangle \)</span> be a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span>. In the following we use the symbols <i>L</i>, <i>R</i>, <i>N</i>, and <i>B</i> as abbreviations for <i>left</i>, <i>right</i>, <i>none</i>, and <i>both</i>, respectively. We say that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu \rangle \)</span> <i>has embedding type</i> (for short, <i>has Type</i> or <i>is of Type</i>) <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle s\_{vis}, spine\_{vis},t\_{vis}\rangle \)</span>, with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s\_{vis},t\_{vis} \in \{L,R,N\}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(spine\_{vis} \in \{L,R,B,N\}\)</span>, where: </p><ol class="u-list-style-none">
                  <li>
                    <span class="u-custom-list-number">1.</span>
                    
                      <p><span class="mathjax-tex">\(s\_{vis}\)</span> is <i>L</i> (resp., <i>R</i>), if in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu )\)</span> there is a portion of the spine incident to <i>s</i> and between <i>s</i> and <i>t</i> that is visible from the left page (resp., from the right page). If <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s\_{vis}\)</span> is neither <i>L</i> nor <i>R</i>, then <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s\_{vis}\)</span> is <i>N</i>.</p>
                    
                  </li>
                  <li>
                    <span class="u-custom-list-number">2.</span>
                    
                      <p><span class="mathjax-tex">\(t\_{vis}\)</span> is <i>L</i> (resp., <i>R</i>), if in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu )\)</span> there is a portion of the spine incident to <i>t</i> and between <i>s</i> and <i>t</i> that is visible from the left page (resp., from the right page). If <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t\_{vis}\)</span> is neither <i>L</i> nor <i>R</i>, then <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t\_{vis}\)</span> is <i>N</i>.</p>
                    
                  </li>
                  <li>
                    <span class="u-custom-list-number">3.</span>
                    
                      <p><span class="mathjax-tex">\(spine\_{vis}\)</span> is <i>L</i> (resp., <i>R</i>), if in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu )\)</span> there is a portion of the spine between <i>s</i> and <i>t</i> that is visible from the left page (resp., from the right page); <span class="mathjax-tex">\(spine\_{vis}\)</span> is <i>B</i>, if in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu )\)</span> there is a portion of the spine between <i>s</i> and <i>t</i> that is visible from the left page, and a portion of the spine between <i>s</i> and <i>t</i> that is visible from the right page. If <span class="mathjax-tex">\(spine\_{vis}\)</span> is neither <i>L</i>, nor <i>R</i>, nor <i>B</i>, then <span class="mathjax-tex">\(spine\_{vis}\)</span> is <i>N</i>.</p>
                    
                  </li>
                </ol><p>Further, we say that a node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> <i>admit embedding type</i> (for short, <i>admit Type</i>) <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle s\_{vis}, spine\_{vis},t\_{vis}\rangle \)</span>, if <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> admits a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of Type <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle s\_{vis}, spine\_{vis},t\_{vis}\rangle \)</span>; in this case, we also say that the Type <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle s\_{vis}, spine\_{vis},t\_{vis}\rangle \)</span> is <i>realizable</i> by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> and by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span>. We have the following.</p>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar21">Lemma 4</h3>
                  <p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> be a node of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\mathcal {T}}}\)</span> and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu \rangle \)</span> be a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span>.</p>
                  <p>Then, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu \rangle \)</span> has exactly one embedding type, where the possible embedding types are the 18 depicted in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig12">12</a>.</p>
                
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar22">Proof</h3>
                  <p>The first part of the statement follows from the definition of embedding type. To see that the number of possible embedding types allowed by a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> is at most 18, it suffices to consider the following facts. First, the number of different embedding types admitted by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> is at most 36. Further, some combinations are “impossible”, in the sense that not all combinations of values for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s\_{vis}\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(spine\_{vis}\)</span>, and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t\_{vis}\)</span> appear in a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span>. In particular, we have that the following values for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(spine\_{vis}\)</span> are forbidden: (a) <span class="mathjax-tex">\(spine\_{vis}\)</span> cannot be <i>L</i>, if either <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s\_{vis}=R\)</span> or <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t\_{vis}=R\)</span>; (b) <span class="mathjax-tex">\(spine\_{vis}\)</span> cannot be <i>R</i>, if either <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s\_{vis}=L\)</span> or <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t\_{vis}=L\)</span>; (c) <span class="mathjax-tex">\(spine\_{vis}\)</span> cannot be <i>N</i>, if either <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s\_{vis} \ne N\)</span> or <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t\_{vis} \ne N\)</span>. Condition a and Condition b rule out 5 combinations each, while Condition c rules out 8 more combinations (see also Table <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="table anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Tab1">1</a>). This leaves us with the 18 embedding types shown in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig12">12</a>. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
                <div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-12" data-title="Fig. 12"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig12" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 12</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/12" rel="nofollow"><picture><source type="image/webp" srcset="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig12_HTML.png?as=webp"><img aria-describedby="figure-12-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig12_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 12" loading="lazy" width="685" height="846"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-12-desc"><p>Illustration of the possible embedding types of a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> for a node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> with poles <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span>; the portions of the spine that are visible from the left page or from the right page are green. Embedding types that are in the same dotted box are one the vertically-mirrored copy of the other. Embedding types on the left are the horizontally-mirrored copies of the ones on the right. Embedding types <img src="//media.springernature.com/lw91/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Figa_HTML.gif" style="width:91px;max-width:none;" alt=""> and <img src="//media.springernature.com/lw92/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Figb_HTML.gif" style="width:92px;max-width:none;" alt=""> are the horizontally-mirrored and vertically-mirrored copies of themselves</p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/12" data-track-dest="link:Figure12 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 12" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div><p>Recall that, for each node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> may have exponentially many planar embeddings, given by the permutations of the children of the P-nodes and by the flips of the R-nodes. Moreover, there may exist exponentially many <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBEs associated with each such planar embedding. The next lemma will be the key tool to help us control this combinatorial explosion.</p><p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span> be a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <i>G</i>, let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> be a node of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span>, and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu \rangle \)</span> be the restriction of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span>. Also, let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi '_\mu , \sigma '_\mu \rangle \ne \langle \pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu \rangle \)</span> be a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span>. We prove the following.</p>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar23">Lemma 5</h3>
                  <p>If <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi '_\mu , \sigma '_\mu \rangle \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu \rangle \)</span> have the same embedding type, then <i>G</i> admits a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE whose restriction to <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> is <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi '_\mu , \sigma '_\mu \rangle \)</span>.</p>
                <div class="c-article-table" data-test="inline-table" data-container-section="table" id="table-1"><figure><figcaption class="c-article-table__figcaption"><b id="Tab1" data-test="table-caption">Table 1 Embedding types that are not possible</b></figcaption><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="table-link" data-track="click" data-track-action="view table" data-track-label="button" rel="nofollow" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/tables/1" aria-label="Full size table 1"><span>Full size table</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar24">Proof</h3>
                  <p>Notice that if <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> is an edge, the statement does not apply, because <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> does not admit two distinct <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBEs of the same type. Thus we assume that <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> has at least three vertices. We show how to construct a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi ', \sigma ' \rangle \)</span> of <i>G</i> whose restriction to <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> is <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi '_\mu , \sigma '_\mu \rangle \)</span>.</p>
                  <p>For the ease of description we actually show how to construct an upward planar drawing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma '\)</span> of <i>G</i> in which each vertex <i>v</i> lies along the spine in the same bottom-to-top order determined by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi '\)</span> and each edge is drawn on the page assigned by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\sigma '\)</span>. Clearly, such a drawing implies the existence of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi ', \sigma ' \rangle \)</span>.</p>
                  <p>Consider a drawing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi ,\sigma )\)</span> of <i>G</i>; refer to Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig13">13</a>a. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{{\overline{\mu }}}\)</span> be the subgraph of <i>G</i> composed of the edges of <i>G</i> not in <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> and of their endpoints. Consider the drawing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma _{{\overline{\mu }}}\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{{\overline{\mu }}}\)</span> obtained by restricting <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi ,\sigma )\)</span> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{{\overline{\mu }}}\)</span>. Denote by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _{{\overline{\mu }}}\)</span> the bottom-to-top order of the vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{{\overline{\mu }}}\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma _{{\overline{\mu }}}\)</span>. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _1, \pi _2, \dots , \pi _k\)</span> be the maximal subsequences of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _{{\overline{\mu }}}\)</span> between <i>s</i> and <i>t</i> and composed of consecutive vertices in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _{{\overline{\mu }}}\)</span> that are also consecutive in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span> (refer to Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig13">13</a>a). Observe that sequences <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _i\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=1, \dots , k\)</span>, may be formed by a single vertex or by multiple vertices. Also, the first sequence <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _1\)</span> includes <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span> and the last sequence <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _k\)</span> includes <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span>.
