Further, since <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> has at least three vertices, there exists at least a vertex of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_{\mu }\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{\mu }\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi ,\sigma )\)</span>; therefore we have that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k \ge 2\)</span>.</p><div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-13" data-title="Fig. 13"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig13" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 13</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/13" rel="nofollow"><picture><source type="image/webp" srcset="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig13_HTML.png?as=webp"><img aria-describedby="figure-13-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig13_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 13" loading="lazy" width="685" height="353"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-13-desc"><p>Illustrations for Lemma <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar23">5</a>. The <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBEs of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> are of Type <img src="//media.springernature.com/lw88/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Figc_HTML.gif" style="width:88px;max-width:none;" alt=""></p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/13" data-track-dest="link:Figure13 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 13" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div>
                  <p>Drawing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma _{{\overline{\mu }}}\)</span> contains a face <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_\mu \)</span> that is incident to the ending vertex of the sequence <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _1\)</span>, to the starting vertex of the sequence <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _k\)</span>, and, if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k \ge 3\)</span>, to all the starting and ending vertices of the sequences <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _i\)</span>, with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i = 2, \dots , k-1\)</span>. Face <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_\mu \)</span> always contains the vertices <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_{\mu }\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{\mu }\)</span>. Some starting and ending vertices of the sequences <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _i\)</span> are encountered when traversing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_\mu \)</span> clockwise from <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span> (<i>left vertices of </i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_\mu \)</span>) and some of them are encountered when traversing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_\mu \)</span> counter-clockwise from <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span> (<i>right vertices of </i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_\mu \)</span>). The left (resp. right) vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_{\mu }\)</span> are red (resp. blue) in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig13">13</a>.</p>
                  <p>We show how to insert into face <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_\mu \)</span> the drawing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi '_\mu ,\sigma '_\mu )\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span>, producing the desired drawing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma '\)</span> of the <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi ', \sigma ' \rangle \)</span> of <i>G</i>. First, consider the drawing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi '_\mu ,\sigma '_\mu )\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> (see Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig13">13</a>b) and insert it, possibly after squeezing it, into <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_\mu \)</span> in such a way that its spine lays entirely on the line of the spine of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma _{{\overline{\mu }}}\)</span> and in such a way that the vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> do not fall in between the vertices of any maximal sequence <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _i\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=1, \dots , k\)</span>. This is always possible, because <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k \ge 2\)</span> and there exists a portion of the spine of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma _{{\overline{\mu }}}\)</span> that is in the interior of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_\mu \)</span>. Observe that this implies that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span> have now a double representation, since the drawing of the source <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s'_\mu \)</span> and sink <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'_\mu \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi '_\mu ,\sigma '_\mu )\)</span> do not coincide with the drawing of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma _{{\overline{\mu }}}\)</span>. Denote by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma ^*\)</span> the resulting drawing (refer to Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig13">13</a>c).</p>
                  <p>Suppose <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu \rangle \)</span> (and, hence, also <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi '_\mu , \sigma '_\mu \rangle \)</span>) is of Type <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle x, y, z \rangle \)</span>. Observe that if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y \in \{N, R\}\)</span> then there are no left vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_\mu \)</span>. Otherwise, if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y \in \{L,B\}\)</span>, then it is possible to identify two vertices <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{\ell ,b}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{\ell ,t}\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi '_\mu ,\sigma '_\mu )\)</span> such that there exists a portion of the spine between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{\ell ,b}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{\ell ,t}\)</span> that is visible from left. Consider all the vertices of the sequences <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _i\)</span> whose starting and ending vertices are left vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_\mu \)</span>, and move them between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{\ell ,b}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{\ell ,t}\)</span> along the spine of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma ^*\)</span>, while preserving their relative order. Analogously, if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y \in \{N, L\}\)</span>, there are no right vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_\mu \)</span>. Otherwise, if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y\in \{R, B\}\)</span>, it is possible to identify two vertices <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{r,b}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{r,t}\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi '_\mu ,\sigma '_\mu )\)</span> such that there exists a portion of the spine between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{r,b}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{r,t}\)</span> that is visible from right. Consider all the vertices of the sequences <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _i\)</span> whose starting and ending vertices are right vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_\mu \)</span>, and move them between <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{r,b}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v_{r,t}\)</span> along the spine of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma ^*\)</span>, while preserving their relative order.</p>
                  <p><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma ^*\)</span> is an upward planar drawing of <i>G</i> except for the fact that there are two duplicated vertices <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s'_\mu \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'_\mu \)</span>. Thus, by identifying <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span> with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s'_\mu \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span> with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'_\mu \)</span>, we obtain the desired upward drawing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma '\)</span> of <i>G</i> in which each vertex <i>v</i> lies along the spine and each edge is drawn as a <i>y</i>-monotone curve on one of the two half-planes defined by the spine. Next we prove that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma '\)</span> is planar. Clearly, identifying <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span> with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s'_\mu \)</span> (<span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span> with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'_\mu \)</span>) does not introduce crossings when <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s'_\mu \)</span> (<span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'_\mu \)</span>) are consecutive along the spine in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma ^*\)</span> (see <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'_\mu \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span> in Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig13">13</a>c). In the case in which <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s'_\mu \)</span> (<span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t'_\mu \)</span>) are not consecutive along the spine in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma ^*\)</span>, necessarily <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x \in \{L,R\}\)</span> (<span class="mathjax-tex">\(z \in \{L,R\}\)</span>). Suppose <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x = L\)</span> (<span class="mathjax-tex">\(z=L\)</span>), the case when <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x = R\)</span> (<span class="mathjax-tex">\(z=R\)</span>) being analogous. There cannot exist in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma _{{\overline{\mu }}}\)</span> an edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\(e=(a,b)\)</span> such that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi (a)< \pi (s_\mu ) < \pi (b)\)</span> and such that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\sigma (e)=R\)</span>. Therefore, we can continuously move <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s'_\mu \)</span>, together with its incident edges, toward <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span>, remaining inside the region bounded by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(f_\mu \)</span> without intersecting any edge of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(G_{{\overline{\mu }}}\)</span> (see Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig13">13</a>d). This concludes the proof. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
                <p>We now describe an algorithm to decide if <i>G</i> admits a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE and its running time. The same procedure can be easily refined to actually compute a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <i>G</i>, with no additional cost, by decorating each node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \in {\mathcal {T}}\)</span> with the embedding choices performed at <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span>, for each of its <i>O</i>(1) possible embedding types.</p><p><b>Testing Algorithm.</b> The algorithm is based on visiting the SPQR-tree <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span> of <i>G</i> and on computing, for each non-root node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span>, the set of embedding types realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span>, assuming to have already computed the set of embedding types realizable by each of the children of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span>. Lemma <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar23">5</a> is the reason why we only need to maintain a single <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span>, for each embedding type realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span>, i.e., a constant number of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBEs instead of an exponential number. Since, by Lemmas <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar21">4</a> and <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar23">5</a>, <i>G</i> admits a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE if and only if the pertinent graph of the unique child of the root Q-node admits an embedding type of (at least) one of the 18 possible embedding types, this approach allows us to solve the 2<span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span> problem for <i>G</i>.</p><p>The computation of the set of embedding types realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> is based on whether <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> is an S-, P-, Q-, or an R-node. Recall that the only possible embedding choices for <i>G</i> happen at P- and R-nodes. While the treatment of Q- and S-nodes does not require any modification when considering the variable and the fixed embedding settings, for P- and R-nodes we will discuss how to compute the embedding types that are realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> in both such settings separately. In particular, in the fixed embedding scenario the above characterization needs to additionally satisfy the constraints imposed by the fixed embedding on the skeletons of the P- and R-nodes in <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span>. Note that, a leaf Q-node only admits Type <img src="//media.springernature.com/lw82/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Figd_HTML.gif" style="width:82px;max-width:none;" alt=""> and Type <img src="//media.springernature.com/lw83/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fige_HTML.gif" style="width:83px;max-width:none;" alt="">. Also, combining <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBEs of the two children of an S-node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> always yields a valid <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span>. The next lemma shows how to compute all the embedding types realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span>.</p>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar25">Lemma 6</h3>
                  <p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> be an S-node. The set of embedding types realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> can be computed in <i>O</i>(1) time, both in the fixed and in the variable embedding setting.</p>
                
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar26">Proof</h3>
                  <p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> be an S-node with poles <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_{\mu }\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{\mu }\)</span>. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu '\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu ''\)</span> be the two children of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> with poles <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_{\mu '}\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{\mu '}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_{\mu ''}\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{\mu ''}\)</span>, respectively, where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_{\mu '}=s_{\mu }\)</span>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{\mu '}=s_{\mu ''}\)</span>, and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{\mu ''}=t_{\mu }\)</span>. Clearly, combining each pair of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBEs of the two children of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> always yields a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span>. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle x',y',z' \rangle \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle x'',y'',z'' \rangle \)</span> be any two embedding types realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu '\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu ''\)</span>, respectively. The embedding type <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle x, y, z \rangle \)</span> of any <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> resulting from a series combination of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBEs of the above types can be computed as follows. We have <span class="mathjax-tex">\(x = x'\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(z = z''\)</span>. As for <i>y</i>, we have that: (i) <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y = N\)</span> iff <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y'=y''=N\)</span>; (ii) <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y=L\)</span> if either <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y'=L\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y'' \in \{L,N\}\)</span> or <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y''=L\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y' \in \{L,N\}\)</span>; (iii) <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y=R\)</span> if either <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y'=R\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y'' \in \{R,N\}\)</span> or <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y''=R\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y' \in \{R,N\}\)</span>; (iv) <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y=B\)</span> if at least one of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y'\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(y''\)</span> is <i>B</i> or one of them is <i>L</i> and the other is <i>R</i>. Since there is a constant number of embedding types realizable by the children of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span>, the statement follows. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
                <p>Since P- and R-nodes require a more complex treatment, in the following we only state the lemmas concerning the computation time of the set of realizable embedding types for these nodes. We defer the proofs of such lemmas to the next sections. Specifically, in Subsection <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Sec11">6.5</a> we show how to efficiently compute the set of embedding types realizable by a P-node in the fixed and variable embedding setting (Subsection <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Sec12">6.5.1</a> and Subsection <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Sec13">6.5.2</a>, respectively). We have the following.</p>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar27">Lemma 7</h3>
                  <p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> be a P-node with <i>k</i> children. The set of embedding types realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> can be computed in <i>O</i>(<i>k</i>) time in the fixed embedding setting.</p>
                
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar28">Lemma 8</h3>
                  <p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> be a P-node with <i>k</i> children. The set of embedding types realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> can be computed in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(k^2)\)</span> time in the variable embedding setting.</p>
                <p>We remark that Lemmas <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar25">6</a> and <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar27">7</a> yield a counterpart, in the fixed embedding setting, of the linear-time algorithm by Di Giacomo et al. [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 46" title="Di Giacomo, E., Didimo, W., Liotta, G., Wismath, S.K.: Book embeddability of series-parallel digraphs. Algorithmica 45(4), 531–547 (2006)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR46" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e42001">46</a>] to compute <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBEs of series-parallel graphs, which we formalize in the following statement.</p>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar29">Theorem 7</h3>
                  <p>There exists an <i>O</i>(<i>n</i>)-time algorithm to decide whether an <i>n</i>-vertex series-parallel <i>st</i>-graph admits an embedding-preserving <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE.</p>
                <p>Finally, in  Subsection <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Sec10">6.4</a> we show how to efficiently compute the set of embedding types realizable by an R-node. In particular, we prove the following.</p>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar30">Lemma 9</h3>
                  <p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> be an R-node with <i>k</i> children and whose skeleton has branchwidth <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\mathrm{\beta }\,}}\)</span>. The set of embedding types realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> can be computed in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(2^{O(\beta \log {\beta })}\cdot k\)</span> time, both in the fixed and in the variable embedding setting, provided that a sphere-cut decomposition <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle T, \xi , \Pi \rangle \)</span> of width <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\beta \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}^+(\mu )\)</span> is given.</p>
                <p>By Lemmas <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar25">6</a> to <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar30">9</a>, and since <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span> has <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(|G |)\)</span> size [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 43" title="Di Battista, G., Tamassia, R.: On-line planarity testing. SIAM J. Comput. 25(5), 956–997 (1996)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR43" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e42370">43</a>, <a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 51" title="Didimo, W., Giordano, F., Liotta, G.: Upward spirality and upward planarity testing. SIAM J. Dis. Math. 23(4), 1842–1899 (2009)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR51" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e42374">51</a>], we get the following.</p>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar31">Theorem 8</h3>
                  <p>There exists an <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(2^{O(\beta \log {\beta })}\cdot n + n^2 + g(n))\)</span>-time algorithm to decide if an <i>n</i>-vertex planar <i>st</i>-graph (resp. plane <i>st</i>-graph) of branchwidth <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\beta \)</span> admits a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE (resp. an embedding-preserving <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE), where <i>g</i>(<i>n</i>) is the computation time of a sphere-cut decomposition of an <i>n</i>-vertex plane graph.</p>
                <p>Since <i>g</i>(<i>n</i>) is <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(n^3)\)</span> by the result in [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 87" title="Seymour, P.D., Thomas, R.: Call routing and the ratcatcher. Combinatorica 14(2), 217–241 (1994)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR87" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e42581">87</a>], we get the following.</p>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar32">Corollary 2</h3>
                  <p>There exists an <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(2^{O(\beta \log {\beta })}\cdot n + n^3)\)</span>-time algorithm to decide if an <i>n</i>-vertex planar <i>st</i>-graph (resp. plane <i>st</i>-graph) of branchwidth <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\beta \)</span> admits a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE (resp. an embedding-preserving <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE).</p>
                  <p>Furthermore, since for any <i>n</i>-vertex planar graph, it holds <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\beta \le 2.122\sqrt{n}\)</span> [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 60" title="Fomin, F.V., Thilikos, D.M.: New upper bounds on the decomposability of planar graphs. J. Graph Theory 51(1), 53–81 (2006)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR60" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e42756">60</a>], Corollary <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar32">2</a> implies that the 2<span class="u-small-caps">UBE Testing</span> problem is solvable in sub-exponential time.</p>
                
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar33">Corollary 3</h3>
                  <p>There exists an <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(2^{O(\sqrt{n} \log {\sqrt{n}})} + n^3)\)</span>-time algorithm to decide if an <i>n</i>-vertex planar <i>st</i>-graph (resp. plane <i>st</i>-graph) admits a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE (resp. an embedding-preserving <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE).</p>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec10"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">6.4 </span>Computing the Embedding Types when <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> is an R-node</h3><p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> be an R-node with poles <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_{\mu }\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_{\mu }\)</span>; refer to Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig14">14</a>. We consider the graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}^+(\mu ) = {{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu ) \cup (s_\mu ,t_\mu )\)</span>, which is a triconnected planar graph. Since <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}^+(\mu )\)</span> is a minor of <i>G</i>, its branchwidth is bounded by the branchwidth <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\beta \)</span> of <i>G</i> [<a data-track="click" data-track-action="reference anchor" data-track-label="link" data-test="citation-ref" aria-label="Reference 86" title="Robertson, N., Seymour, P.D.: Graph minors. x. obstructions to tree-decomposition. J. Comb. Theory, Ser. B, 52(2):153–190,( 1991)" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#ref-CR86" id="ref-link-section-d6319272e43146">86</a>, Theorem 4.1]. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {E}}_\mu \)</span> be an embedding of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}^+(\mu )\)</span> on the sphere and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle T, \xi , \Pi \rangle \)</span> be a sphere-cut decomposition of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}^+(\mu )\)</span> with embedding <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {E}}_\mu \)</span> of width smaller than or equal to <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\mathrm{\beta }\,}}\)</span>; refer to Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig14">14</a>a.</p><p>We root <i>T</i> at the leaf <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\rho \)</span> of <i>T</i> such that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\xi (\rho ) = (s_\mu ,t_\mu )\)</span>. Consider each arc <span class="mathjax-tex">\(a=(p_a,c_a)\)</span> of <i>T</i> connecting the parent node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_a\)</span> with the child node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(c_a\)</span> in <i>T</i>. The noose <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O_a\)</span> of <i>a</i> divides the sphere into two closed regions whose boundary is <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O_a\)</span>. We associate <i>a</i> with the subgraph <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_a\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}^+(\mu )\)</span> lying in the region not containing the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_\mu ,t_\mu )\)</span>, and we refer to the face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_a\)</span> that contains such an edge as the <i>outer face</i> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_a\)</span>. We denote by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}_a\)</span> the subgraph of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> obtained by replacing, in <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_a\)</span>, each virtual edge <i>e</i> with the pertinent graph of the node corresponding to <i>e</i> in the SPQR-tree <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span> of <i>G</i>.</p><p>Intuitively, our strategy to compute the embedding types admitted by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> is to visit the nodes of <i>T</i> in post order. When a node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(c_a\)</span> incident to the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\(a = (p_a,c_a)\)</span> is visited, we compute a succinct description of size <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O({{\,\mathrm{\beta }\,}})\)</span> of the properties of the noose <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O_a\)</span> in a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}_a\)</span>. Note that the arc <span class="mathjax-tex">\(a^*\)</span> incident to the root <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\rho \)</span> is such that <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_{a^*}\)</span> (resp. <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}_{a^*}\)</span>) coincides with <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span> (resp. with <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span>). When we reach <span class="mathjax-tex">\(a^*\)</span>, we use the computed properties for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O_{a^*}\)</span> to determine which embedding types are realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span>. The fixed and the variable embedding setting are treated analogously. In the fixed embedding setting, we only consider the embedding of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}^+(\mu )\)</span> in which the embedding of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span> is inherited by the (fixed) embedding of <i>G</i>. In the variable embedding setting, instead, we consider each of the two embeddings of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}^+(\mu )\)</span> obtained by flipping the embedding of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span> at its poles. </p><div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-14" data-title="Fig. 14"><figure><figcaption><b id="Fig14" class="c-article-section__figure-caption" data-test="figure-caption-text">Fig. 14</b></figcaption><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><a class="c-article-section__figure-link" data-test="img-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="image" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/14" rel="nofollow"><picture><source type="image/webp" srcset="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig14_HTML.png?as=webp"><img aria-describedby="figure-14-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Fig14_HTML.png" alt="Fig. 14" loading="lazy" width="685" height="600"></picture></a></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-14-desc"><p>Illustrations for the R-node case. <b>a</b> A partial sphere-cut decomposition of the graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu ) \cup (s_\mu ,t_\mu )\)</span> rooted at the edge <span class="mathjax-tex">\((s_\mu ,t_\mu )\)</span>, where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> is an R-node. The nooses are dotted curves. <b>b</b> Outer faces of graphs <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_{a_1}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_{a_2}\)</span>. <b>c</b> The graphs defined by the directed paths on the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_{a_1}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_{a_2}\)</span>. <b>d</b> The auxiliary graph <i>A</i> for <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_{a}\)</span></p></div></div><div class="u-text-right u-hide-print"><a class="c-article__pill-button" data-test="article-link" data-track="click" data-track-label="button" data-track-action="view figure" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y/figures/14" data-track-dest="link:Figure14 Full size image" aria-label="Full size image figure 14" rel="nofollow"><span>Full size image</span><svg width="16" height="16" focusable="false" role="img" aria-hidden="true" class="u-icon"><use xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="#icon-eds-i-chevron-right-small"></use></svg></a></div></figure></div><p>Let <i>a</i> be an arc of <i>T</i> and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_0, p_1, \dots , p_k\)</span> be the sequence of maximal (upward or downward) directed paths traversed in a clockwise visit of the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_a\)</span>. The next lemma shows that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(k \in O(\beta )\)</span>.</p>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar34">Lemma 10</h3>
                  <p>For each arc <span class="mathjax-tex">\(a \in T\)</span>, by clockwise visiting the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_a\)</span>, we traverse at most <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O({{\,\mathrm{\beta }\,}})\)</span> maximal (upward or downward) directed paths.</p>
                
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar35">Proof</h3>
                  <p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_{u,v}\)</span> be the path along the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_a\)</span> connecting two vertices of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{mid}\,}}(a)\)</span> that are clockwise consecutive in the circular ordering <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _a \in \Pi \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{mid}\,}}(a)\)</span>. By Property <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar19">3</a>, <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> is an <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu t_\mu \)</span>-graph, and thus its faces have a single source and a single sink. If <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_{u,v}\)</span> was composed of more than three maximal (upward or downward) directed paths, then the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_a\)</span> would contain either two sources or two sinks belonging to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_{u,v}\)</span>. The statement then follows from the fact that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(|{{\,\textrm{mid}\,}}(a) |\le {{\,\mathrm{\beta }\,}}\)</span>. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
                <p>Let <i>u</i> and <i>v</i> be two clockwise consecutive vertices along the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_a\)</span> that are connected by an (upward or downward) directed path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_i\)</span>. Given a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _a, \sigma _a\rangle \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}_a\)</span>, we associate with <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_i\)</span> an <i>outer-visibility triple </i><span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_i = \langle u\_{vis}, spine\_{vis}, v\_{vis} \rangle \)</span> <i>of </i><span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_i\)</span><i> in </i> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _a, \sigma _a\rangle \)</span>, for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i = 1, \dots , k\)</span>, which encodes the information about the visibility of the spine between <i>u</i> and <i>v</i> from the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi _a, \sigma _a)\)</span>. In particular, we have: </p><dl class="c-abbreviation_list"><dt class="c-abbreviation_list__term u-text-bold u-float-left u-pr-16" style="min-width:50px;"><dfn><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mathbf {u\_{vis}=true}\)</span>:</dfn></dt><dd class="c-abbreviation_list__description u-mb-24">
                      <p>if and only if there is a portion of the spine incident to <i>u</i> and between <i>u</i> and <i>v</i> that is visible from the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi _a, \sigma _a)\)</span>.</p>
                    </dd><dt class="c-abbreviation_list__term u-text-bold u-float-left u-pr-16" style="min-width:50px;"><dfn><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mathbf {v\_{vis}=true}\)</span>:</dfn></dt><dd class="c-abbreviation_list__description u-mb-24">
                      <p>if and only if there is a portion of the spine incident to <i>v</i> and between <i>u</i> and <i>v</i> that is visible from the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi _a, \sigma _a)\)</span>.</p>
                    </dd><dt class="c-abbreviation_list__term u-text-bold u-float-left u-pr-16" style="min-width:50px;"><dfn><span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mathbf {spine\_{vis}=true}\)</span>:</dfn></dt><dd class="c-abbreviation_list__description u-mb-24">
                      <p>if and only if there is a portion of the spine between <i>u</i> and <i>v</i> that is visible from the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi _a, \sigma _a)\)</span>.</p>
                    </dd></dl><p> Observe that when <span class="mathjax-tex">\(u\_{vis} = true\)</span> or <span class="mathjax-tex">\(v\_{vis} = true\)</span>, then necessarily <span class="mathjax-tex">\(spine\_{vis} = true\)</span>. Hence, we have five possible types for the outer-visibility triples <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle u\_{vis}, spine\_{vis}, v\_{vis} \rangle \)</span>.</p><p>We denote by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(X_a\)</span> the set of all the endpoints of the paths <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_1,\dots ,p_k\)</span>. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu \rangle \)</span> be a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span>, let <i>a</i> be an arc of <i>T</i>, and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _a, \sigma _a\rangle \)</span> be the restriction of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu \rangle \)</span> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}_a\)</span>. Further, let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi '_a, \sigma '_a\rangle \ne \langle \pi _a, \sigma _a\rangle \)</span> be a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}_a\)</span>. The next lemma can be proved with the same strategy as in the proof of Lemma <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar23">5</a>.</p>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar36">Lemma 11</h3>
                  <p>Graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> admits a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi '_\mu , \sigma '_\mu \rangle \)</span> whose restriction to <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}_a\)</span> is <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi '_a, \sigma '_a\rangle \)</span> if the following two conditions hold: 1.the bottom-to-top order of the vertices in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(X_a\)</span> is the same in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _a\)</span> as in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi '_a\)</span>, and 2.for each <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=1,\dots ,k\)</span>, the outer-visibility triple of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_i\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi '_a, \sigma '_a)\)</span> is the same as the outer-visibility triple of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_i\)</span> in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi _a, \sigma _a)\)</span>.</p>
                <p>When visiting <i>T</i> bottom-up, we compute and store in each arc <i>a</i> of <i>T</i> those pairs <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\pi _a, \langle t_1, t_2, \dots , t_k \rangle )\)</span>, called <i>outer-shape pairs</i>, where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _a\)</span> is a bottom-to-top order of the vertices in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(X_a\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_i\)</span> is an outer-visibility triple for path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_i\)</span>, for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=1,\dots ,k\)</span>, such that there exists a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}_a\)</span> satisfying the following properties: 1. ordering <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _a\)</span> is the restriction of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi \)</span> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(X_a\)</span> and 2. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_i\)</span> is the outer-visibility triple determined by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi , \sigma \rangle \)</span> when clockwise traversing the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi , \sigma )\)</span> between the endpoints of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_i\)</span>. By Lemma <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar36">11</a>, this information is enough to succinctly describe all the relevant properties of the <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBEs  of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}_a\)</span>. Moreover, by Property <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar19">3</a><span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_{a^*}={{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span> is bounded by two directed paths <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_1\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_2\)</span> whose end-vertices are <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span>. Thus, when we reach the arc <span class="mathjax-tex">\(a^*\)</span>, all the embedding types that are realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> can be computed by inspecting the outer-visibility triples <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_1\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_2\)</span>, over all outer-shape pairs <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\pi _a, \langle t_1, t_2 \rangle )\)</span> stored in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(a^*\)</span>.</p><p>Next, we show how to compute all outer-shape pairs <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\pi _a, \langle t_1, t_2, \dots , t_k \rangle )\)</span>, for each arc <span class="mathjax-tex">\(a \in T\)</span>.</p><p>Suppose <i>a</i> leads to a leaf <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\ell \)</span> of <i>T</i> such that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\xi (\ell ) = (u,v)\)</span>. Assume that <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}_a\)</span> is oriented from <i>u</i> to <i>v</i> and that the clockwise boundary of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_a\)</span> is composed by the upward path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_1 = u,v\)</span> and the downward path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_2 = v,u\)</span>, the other case being analogous. Then, all the outer-shape pairs <span class="mathjax-tex">\((\pi _a, \langle t_1, t_2 \rangle )\)</span> for <i>a</i> are such that <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\pi _a = u,v\)</span>, since <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}_a\)</span> is an <i>uv</i>-graph. Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu _\ell \)</span> be the child of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> in the SPQR-tree <span class="mathjax-tex">\({\mathcal {T}}\)</span> associated with the virtual edge (<i>u</i>, <i>v</i>). We compute the outer-visibility triples <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_1=\langle \alpha , \varphi , \gamma \rangle \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_2=\langle \alpha ', \varphi ', \gamma ' \rangle \)</span> for <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_1\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_2\)</span>, respectively, starting from the embedding types realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu _\ell \)</span>, as follows. For each embedding type <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle s\_{vis},spine\_{vis},t\_{vis} \rangle \)</span> of the node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu _\ell \)</span> we define <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_1\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_2\)</span> as follows. If <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s\_{vis} = L\)</span>, we set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha = true\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha ' = false\)</span>. If <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s\_{vis} = R\)</span> we set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha = false\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha ' = true\)</span>. If <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s\_{vis} = N\)</span> we set both <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\alpha = \alpha ' = false\)</span>. Analogously, we set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\varphi \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\varphi '\)</span> according to the value of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t\_{vis}\)</span>. We set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma = true\)</span> if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(spine\_{vis} = B\)</span> or <span class="mathjax-tex">\(spine\_{vis} = L\)</span>. Otherwise, we set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma = false\)</span>. Finally, we set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma ' = true\)</span> if <span class="mathjax-tex">\(spine\_{vis} = B\)</span> or <span class="mathjax-tex">\(spine\_{vis} = R\)</span>. Otherwise, we set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\gamma ' = false\)</span>.</p><p>Suppose now that <i>a</i> leads to a non-leaf node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\lambda \)</span> of <i>T</i>. Then, <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\lambda \)</span> has two children <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\lambda _1\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\lambda _2\)</span>, reached by two arcs <span class="mathjax-tex">\(a_1\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(a_2\)</span> of <i>T</i>, for which we have already computed all the outer-shape pairs. We are going to use the following observation, which follows from the planarity of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span> and from the fact that the nooses are pairwise non-crossing.</p>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar37">Observation 1</h3>
                  <p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu \rangle \)</span> be a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span>, and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _{a_1}, \sigma _{a_1} \rangle \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _{a_2}, \sigma _{a_2} \rangle \)</span> be the <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBEs of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_{a_1}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_{a_2}\)</span>, respectively, determined by <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _\mu , \sigma _\mu \rangle \)</span>. The following two properties hold: 1. the edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_{a_1}\)</span> lie in the outer face of the drawing obtained by restricting <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi _\mu ,\sigma _\mu )\)</span> to <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_{a_2}\)</span>, and vice versa, and 2. the edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_{a_1}\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_{a_2}\)</span> do not interleave around a vertex in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\Gamma (\pi _\mu ,\sigma _\mu )\)</span>.</p>
                <p>For <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=1,2\)</span>, let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(A_i=(V_i,E_i)\)</span> be the <i>auxiliary (multi)-graph</i> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}_{a_i}\)</span> defined as follows. Initialize <span class="mathjax-tex">\(V_i=X_{a_i}\)</span> and add to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(E_i\)</span> a directed edge (<i>u</i>, <i>v</i>) for each path <i>p</i> directed from <i>u</i> to <i>v</i> traversed when clockwise visiting the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_{a_i}\)</span>; see Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig14">14</a>c. Then, replace each directed edge (<i>u</i>, <i>v</i>) with a directed path <span class="mathjax-tex">\((u,x',x'',v)\)</span>, unless (<i>u</i>, <i>v</i>) is an edge in <i>G</i>. Clearly, graph <span class="mathjax-tex">\(A_i\)</span> is connected. Note that, there exists a one-to-one correspondence between the directed paths along the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}_{a_i}\)</span> (see Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig14">14</a>b) and the directed paths along the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(A_i\)</span> (see Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig14">14</a>c). We have that each <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(A_i\)</span> defines a set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle t_1,\dots ,t_{k_i}\rangle \)</span>, where <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_i\)</span> is an outer-visibility triple describing the visibility of each directed path along the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(A_i\)</span> in the <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE. We define a new graph <i>A</i> as the union of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(A_1\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(A_2\)</span>; see Fig. <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="figure anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Fig14">14</a>d. The purpose of graph <i>A</i> is that of representing the visibility of the spine on the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}_a\)</span> between pairs of vertices in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(X_a\)</span> in a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}_a\)</span>. In particular, by assigning to the faces of the book embedding the three edges that replace each directed path <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_i\)</span> along the outer face of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}_a\)</span>, we are able to model the outer-visibility triples of paths <span class="mathjax-tex">\(p_1, \dots , p_k\)</span>. We compute all outer-shape pairs <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle \pi _a, \langle t_1,\dots ,t_k \rangle \rangle \)</span> for <i>a</i> using Algorithm 1.</p><div class="c-article-section__figure c-article-section__figure--no-border" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-f"><figure><div class="c-article-section__figure-content" id="Figf"><div class="c-article-section__figure-item"><div class="c-article-section__figure-content"><picture><img aria-describedby="figure-f-desc" src="//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs00453-023-01142-y/MediaObjects/453_2023_1142_Figf_HTML.png" alt="figure f" loading="lazy" width="685" height="338"></picture></div></div><div class="c-article-section__figure-description" data-test="bottom-caption" id="figure-f-desc"><p>Procedure to compute the outer-shape pairs of an arc <span class="mathjax-tex">\(a \in T\)</span>.</p></div></div></figure></div><p>We now prove Lemma <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar30">9</a>, which we restate for the reader’s convenience.</p><p><b>Lemma</b> <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar30">9</a> Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> be an R-node with <i>k</i> children and whose skeleton has branchwidth <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\mathrm{\beta }\,}}\)</span>. The set of embedding types realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> can be computed in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2^{O({{\,\mathrm{\beta }\,}}\log {{{\,\mathrm{\beta }\,}}})}\cdot k\)</span> time, both in the fixed and in the variable embedding setting, provided that a sphere-cut decomposition <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle T, \xi , \Pi \rangle \)</span> of width <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\beta \)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}^+(\mu )\)</span> is given.</p>
                  <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="FPar38">Proof</h3>
                  <p>We exploit Algorithm 1 on the arcs of <i>T</i> to compute their outer-shape pairs. As already observed, this allows us to compute the embedding types realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span> from the outer-shape pairs of the arc <span class="mathjax-tex">\(a^*\)</span> incident to the root <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\rho \)</span> of <i>T</i>. The correctness of Algorithm 1 descends from Lemma <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar21">4</a>, Lemma <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar36">11</a> and Observation <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar37">1</a>.</p>
                  <p>We argue about the running time of Algorithm 1 for a given arc <i>a</i> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(T_\mu \)</span>. Note that the auxiliary graph <i>A</i> corresponding to <i>a</i> contains <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(\beta )\)</span> vertices, by Lemma <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar34">10</a>, and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(\beta )\)</span> edges, since it is planar. First, the running time of <b>Step 1</b> is bounded by the number of possible <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBEs  of <i>A</i>. By enumerating all the <span class="mathjax-tex">\(|V(A) |! \in O(\beta !) \in 2^{O(\beta \log {\beta })}\)</span> linear orders of <i>V</i>(<i>A</i>) and all the <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2^{|E(A) |} \in 2^{O(\beta )}\)</span> edge assignments for <i>E</i>(<i>A</i>), we may construct <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2^{O(\beta \log {\beta })}\)</span> <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBEs of <i>A</i>. <b>Step 2</b> is also bounded by the numbers of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBEs of <i>A</i>, as testing whether the conditions of Observation <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="subsection anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#FPar37">1</a> are satisfied by each such a <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBE can be performed in time linear in the size of <i>A</i>, i.e., <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(\beta )\)</span> time. Clearly, for each of the <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2\)</span>UBEs of <i>A</i> considered at <b>Step 3</b>, the remaining steps of Algorithm 1 can also be performed in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(O(\beta )\)</span> time. Thus, Algorithm 1 runs in <span class="mathjax-tex">\(2^{O(\beta \log {\beta })}\)</span> time. The overall running time for computing the embedding types of an R-node <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> follows from the fact that tree <i>T</i> contains <i>O</i>(<i>k</i>) nodes and arcs, since it is a binary tree whose leaves are the <i>k</i> virtual edges of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span>, and from the time spent to compute the outer-shape pairs for each arc of <i>T</i>, i.e., the running time of Algorithm 1. <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\square \)</span></p>
                <h3 class="c-article__sub-heading" id="Sec11"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">6.5 </span>Computing the Embedding Types when <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> is a P-node</h3><p>We show how to efficiently compute the set of embedding types realizable by the pertinent graph of a P-node. The fixed and variable embedding settings are treated in Subsection <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Sec12">6.5.1</a> and Subsection <a data-track="click" data-track-label="link" data-track-action="section anchor" href="/article/10.1007/s00453-023-01142-y#Sec13">6.5.2</a>, respectively.</p><h4 class="c-article__sub-heading c-article__sub-heading--small" id="Sec12"><span class="c-article-section__title-number">6.5.1 </span>P-nodes: Fixed Embedding</h4><p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> be a P-node with poles <span class="mathjax-tex">\(s_\mu \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(t_\mu \)</span> and let <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu _1,\mu _2,\dots ,\mu _k\)</span> be the children of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> in the left-to-right order defined by the given embedding of <i>G</i>.</p><p>Let <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}^i(\mu )\)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}^i(\mu )\)</span> be the subgraphs of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{skel}\,}}(\mu )\)</span> and of <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu })\)</span>, respectively, determined by the children <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu _1,\mu _2,\dots ,\mu _i\)</span> of <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\mu \)</span> (<span class="mathjax-tex">\(1 \le i \le k\)</span>). Since <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}^1(\mu )={{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu _1})\)</span>, the set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(X_1\)</span> of embedding types that are realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}^1(\mu )\)</span> are those that are realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu _1})\)</span>. For <span class="mathjax-tex">\(i=2,\dots ,k\)</span>, we can compute the set <span class="mathjax-tex">\(X_i\)</span> of embedding types that are realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}^i(\mu )\)</span> as follows. We consider each embedding type <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle x,y,z \rangle \in X_{i-1}\)</span> and each embedding type <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle a,b,c\rangle \)</span> that is realizable by <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}({\mu _i})\)</span>, and we either reject the pair, if no embedding type can be obtained by composing <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle x,y,z \rangle \)</span> and <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle a,b,c\rangle \)</span>, or compute an embedding type <span class="mathjax-tex">\(\langle p,q,w\rangle \)</span> for <span class="mathjax-tex">\({{\,\textrm{pert}\,}}^i(\mu )\)</span>, which we add to <span class="mathjax-tex">\(X_i\)</span>. The sequence of the two tests is described below.</p><div class="c-article-section__figure js-c-reading-companion-figures-item" data-test="figure" data-container-section="figure" id="figure-15"
